differentials.clue: The pupil is widely dilated with absent light and near responses; failure to constrict to pilocarpine supports pharmacologic blockade.

differentials.name: corneal ulcer
differentials.clue: An ulcer combines an epithelial defect with a stromal infiltrate, pain, photophobia, and anterior-chamber inflammation; it needs urgent treatment.

differentials.name: dendritic ulcer
differentials.clue: Branching fluorescein stain with terminal bulbs; avoid topical steroids.

differentials.name: retained foreign body
differentials.clue: Evert the upper lid and inspect the cornea and conjunctiva; a subtarsal foreign body may produce repeated vertical epithelial scratches.

differentials.name: positive Seidel test
differentials.clue: Aqueous leakage creates a dark streaming track through fluorescein, indicating a full-thickness corneal or scleral wound.

title: Median nerve pen test
subject: Anatomy / Neurology
source: Pen Test | Median Nerve | How to do the pen test | Abductor policis brevis | abduction of thumb| | Channel: Logic Medico
summary: The close-up shows the thumb moving perpendicular to the palm toward the pen during resisted palmar abduction.
identify: The thumb must move perpendicular to the palm against the pen; a still cannot show the direction or loss of abduction.
examAngle: Palmar abduction is produced mainly by abductor pollicis brevis, supplied by the recurrent motor branch of the median nerve. NEET PG pitfall: Do not credit simple thumb extension or radial abduction as a normal pen test.
management: Treat the level and cause of injury. Carpal-tunnel syndrome may need activity change, night splinting, local corticosteroid injection, or decompression; traumatic motor-branch injury needs early hand or nerve-surgery assessment and rehabilitation.
quickRevision: At 00:10-00:15, watch for: The close-up shows the thumb moving perpendicular to the palm toward the pen during resisted palmar abduction. Core concept: Palmar abduction is produced mainly by abductor pollicis brevis, supplied by the recurrent motor branch of the median nerve. Pitfall: Do not credit simple thumb extension or radial abduction as a normal pen test.
anchors: The movement should leave the plane of the palm; radial abduction within the plane tests a different action.
Compare both hands and look for thenar wasting when median motor neuropathy is suspected.
differentiation: Confirm weak palmar abduction against resistance, then examine thenar bulk, opposition, median sensory loss, and the remaining C8-T1 muscles. Nerve-conduction studies help separate carpal-tunnel compression from a proximal median lesion; isolated recurrent-motor-branch injury may spare sensation.

title: Gag reflex examination
subject: Anatomy / Neurology
source: Cranial Nerves 9 & 10- Sensory and Motor: Gag Reflex 23/25 | Channel: Victor Castilla
summary: The examiner stimulates the posterior pharyngeal wall and the patient produces a visible palatal and pharyngeal response.
identify: The tested value is the pharyngeal response after unilateral stimulation, including symmetry and palatal movement.
examAngle: The sensory limb is mainly glossopharyngeal nerve and the motor limb is mainly vagus nerve. NEET PG pitfall: An absent gag reflex alone does not prove unsafe swallowing or a lower cranial-nerve lesion.
management: Protect the airway and treat the brainstem, neuromuscular, or peripheral cranial-nerve cause. Speech and swallowing therapy, texture modification, supervised feeding, or temporary enteral support may be required when swallowing is unsafe.
quickRevision: At 00:04-00:10, watch for: The examiner stimulates the posterior pharyngeal wall and the patient produces a visible palatal and pharyngeal response. Core concept: The sensory limb is mainly glossopharyngeal nerve and the motor limb is mainly vagus nerve. Pitfall: An absent gag reflex alone does not prove unsafe swallowing or a lower cranial-nerve lesion.
anchors: Test each side separately while observing palatal elevation and pharyngeal contraction.
The gag reflex is variable in healthy adults and must be interpreted with voice, cough, palate, and swallowing findings.
differentiation: Interpret gag asymmetry with palatal elevation, voice, voluntary cough, tongue movement, and swallowing. Because a normal adult may have little or no gag, suspected aspiration requires a formal bedside or instrumental swallow assessment rather than reliance on this reflex alone.

title: Bradykinesia: finger-tapping decrement in Parkinson disease
subject: Medicine / Neurology
source: Symmetric Bradykinesia, Rigidity, and Hypomimia in Patient With Early-Onset Progressive Parkinsonism | Channel: JAMA Network
summary: A real patient repeatedly taps finger and thumb; the taps become slower and smaller across the sequence.
identify: Progressive slowing and reduction in tapping amplitude are temporal findings that cannot be judged from one frame.
examAngle: Parkinsonian bradykinesia requires slowness with decrement in speed or amplitude during repetitive movement. NEET PG pitfall: Tremor interrupting otherwise full-amplitude taps is not the same as a true sequence effect.
management: Levodopa is the most effective symptomatic drug; dopamine agonists, MAO-B inhibitors, and other agents are selected by age and complications. Exercise, physiotherapy, fall prevention, speech and swallow care, and deep-brain stimulation in carefully selected fluctuating disease complete management.
quickRevision: At 00:05-00:20, watch for: A real patient repeatedly taps finger and thumb; the taps become slower and smaller across the sequence. Core concept: Parkinsonian bradykinesia requires slowness with decrement in speed or amplitude during repetitive movement. Pitfall: Tremor interrupting otherwise full-amplitude taps is not the same as a true sequence effect.
anchors: Use a sustained sequence rather than judging the first two or three taps.
Compare sides because early idiopathic Parkinson disease is often asymmetric.
differentiation: Parkinsonian bradykinesia is diagnosed clinically by slowness plus decrement during repetitive movement, together with rest tremor or rigidity. Review dopamine-blocking drugs and look for early falls, gaze palsy, severe autonomic failure, or symmetric lower-body disease that suggests an atypical or secondary syndrome.

title: Cogwheel rigidity in Parkinson disease
subject: Medicine / Neurology
source: Neurology - Topic 14 - Parkinsons disease - examining a patient | Channel: UCD Medicine
summary: At the linked segment, the neurologist passively moves the real patient's wrists and forearms to assess rigidity.
identify: The ratchety interruption during passive movement is revealed only while the examiner repeatedly moves the joint.
examAngle: Rigidity is increased tone throughout passive movement and is not dependent on movement velocity. NEET PG pitfall: Do not confuse cogwheel rigidity with velocity-dependent spasticity and its clasp-knife release.
management: Manage the underlying parkinsonian disorder, usually with levodopa-based treatment and regular exercise or physiotherapy. Review dopamine-blocking medication and address pain, contracture, gait, and fall risk rather than treating rigidity as an isolated diagnosis.
quickRevision: At 06:30-06:45, watch for: At the linked segment, the neurologist passively moves the real patient's wrists and forearms to assess rigidity. Core concept: Rigidity is increased tone throughout passive movement and is not dependent on movement velocity. Pitfall: Do not confuse cogwheel rigidity with velocity-dependent spasticity and its clasp-knife release.
anchors: Cogwheeling is a ratchety interruption of rigidity, often reflecting superimposed tremor.
A contralateral activation maneuver can make subtle rigidity easier to detect.
differentiation: Test tone with the limb fully relaxed and at more than one speed; contralateral activation can reveal subtle rigidity. Velocity-independent lead-pipe or cogwheel tone favors parkinsonism, while velocity-dependent clasp-knife tone favors an upper-motor-neuron lesion.

title: Hemiballismus from a subthalamic lesion
subject: Medicine / Neurology
source: hemiballismus | Channel: neurosigns.org
summary: The real patient has irregular, high-amplitude flinging movements affecting one side, with clear proximal predominance.
identify: The diagnosis depends on irregular, high-amplitude flinging movements and their proximal predominance over time.
examAngle: Hemiballismus classically follows a lesion of the contralateral subthalamic nucleus or its connections. NEET PG pitfall: Do not label every irregular distal dance-like movement as ballismus; amplitude and proximal distribution matter.
management: Correct the cause, especially severe hyperglycemia or acute vascular disease, and protect the patient from injury. Disabling persistent movements may be suppressed with a dopamine receptor blocker or VMAT2 inhibitor under neurologic supervision.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The real patient has irregular, high-amplitude flinging movements affecting one side, with clear proximal predominance. Core concept: Hemiballismus classically follows a lesion of the contralateral subthalamic nucleus or its connections. Pitfall: Do not label every irregular distal dance-like movement as ballismus; amplitude and proximal distribution matter.
anchors: The movements are larger and more proximal than typical distal choreiform movements.
Vascular lesions and nonketotic hyperglycemia are important acquired causes.
differentiation: Confirm unilateral, irregular, high-amplitude proximal flinging and distinguish it from chorea, dystonia, tremor, focal motor seizure, and functional movement. Check capillary glucose urgently and obtain brain imaging for contralateral subthalamic or related basal-ganglia pathology.

title: Pronator drift after ischemic stroke
subject: Medicine / Neurology
source: Right arm pronator drift following stroke | Channel: Dr Gauhar Mahmood Azeem
summary: With both arms held forward and palms up, the affected arm gradually pronates and drifts downward.
identify: The affected arm progressively pronates and descends while both arms are held out; the direction and evolution over seconds are the diagnostic information.
examAngle: Pronator drift is a sensitive sign of mild corticospinal weakness in the upper limb. NEET PG pitfall: Do not call simple fatigue or symmetric lowering of both arms a positive pronator-drift test.
management: Activate the acute-stroke pathway when onset is sudden, including reperfusion assessment and vascular imaging within the appropriate window. Later care targets the cause and combines antithrombotic or vascular prevention with occupational and motor rehabilitation.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: With both arms held forward and palms up, the affected arm gradually pronates and drifts downward. Core concept: Pronator drift is a sensitive sign of mild corticospinal weakness in the upper limb. Pitfall: Do not call simple fatigue or symmetric lowering of both arms a positive pronator-drift test.
anchors: A unilateral drift usually points to a contralateral cerebral corticospinal lesion or an ipsilateral cervical cord lesion, depending on the rest of the examination.
Upward or wandering drift without pronation suggests impaired proprioception; cerebellar disease may produce an irregular outward drift.
differentiation: A reproducible unilateral pronating downward drift is interpreted with power, reflexes, sensation, coordination, face, and leg findings to localize the lesion. Sudden onset is a focal neurologic deficit and requires urgent glucose testing and stroke imaging.

title: Ankle reflex examination
subject: Medicine / Neurology
source: Neurologic Examination of the Foot: Ankle Reflexes | Channel: 360WoundCare
summary: The examiner dorsiflexes the foot slightly and taps the Achilles tendon to elicit the ankle jerk.
identify: The tendon strike and resulting plantar-flexion response must be seen in sequence to judge presence and amplitude.
examAngle: The ankle reflex primarily tests the S1 root through the tibial nerve. NEET PG pitfall: Always compare both sides and document whether reinforcement was required.
management: There is no treatment for the reflex itself. Treat the identified radiculopathy, neuropathy, metabolic disorder, or cord lesion and address weakness, gait instability, pain, and fall risk with rehabilitation as appropriate.
quickRevision: At 00:12-00:27, watch for: The examiner dorsiflexes the foot slightly and taps the Achilles tendon to elicit the ankle jerk. Core concept: The ankle reflex primarily tests the S1 root through the tibial nerve. Pitfall: Always compare both sides and document whether reinforcement was required.
anchors: Jendrassik reinforcement can reveal a reflex that is difficult to obtain in a tense patient.
A reduced ankle jerk supports peripheral neuropathy or S1 radiculopathy; a brisk response supports an upper motor neuron lesion.
differentiation: The ankle jerk tests the S1-S2 reflex arc through the tibial nerve, predominantly S1. A reduced response suggests S1 radiculopathy, tibial neuropathy, or generalized neuropathy; a brisk response with clonus supports corticospinal disease, but technique and relaxation must first be adequate.

title: Constrictive pericarditis: rapid y descent in the JVP
subject: Medicine / Cardiology
source: Jugular Venous Pulsations in Constrictive Pericarditis | Channel: JAMA Network
summary: The neck close-up shows a brisk inward y descent recurring with each cardiac cycle in constrictive physiology.
identify: A rapid y descent is a phasic venous motion timed to the cardiac cycle, not a static neck-vein appearance.
examAngle: Constrictive pericarditis permits rapid early ventricular filling followed by abrupt restraint. NEET PG pitfall: Do not mistake the palpable, single-upstroke carotid pulse for the nonpalpable biphasic JVP.
management: Use cautious diuresis for congestion while avoiding excessive preload reduction. Definitive treatment for symptomatic chronic constrictive pericarditis is complete pericardiectomy when operative risk and etiology permit; transient inflammatory constriction may respond to anti-inflammatory therapy.
quickRevision: At 00:02-00:14, watch for: The neck close-up shows a brisk inward y descent recurring with each cardiac cycle in constrictive physiology. Core concept: Constrictive pericarditis permits rapid early ventricular filling followed by abrupt restraint. Pitfall: Do not mistake the palpable, single-upstroke carotid pulse for the nonpalpable biphasic JVP.
anchors: A prominent y descent and Kussmaul sign support constriction when the rest of the examination fits.
The y descent is typically blunted or absent in cardiac tamponade.
differentiation: Combine a rapid y descent with elevated JVP, Kussmaul sign, pericardial knock, preserved or near-preserved ejection fraction, and respiratory ventricular interdependence. Echocardiography, CT or MRI for pericardial disease, and catheterization when uncertainty remains distinguish constriction from restrictive cardiomyopathy and tamponade.

title: Constrictive pericarditis: respiratory septal bounce
subject: Medicine / Cardiology
source: Septal bounce (Constrictive Pericarditis) | Channel: Dr. Yemin Ahmed
summary: The echocardiographic clip demonstrates respiratory interventricular dependence with a septal bounce.
identify: Septal shift changes with respiration and must be followed across several beats on cine echocardiography.
examAngle: Constrictive pericarditis limits total cardiac volume and exaggerates ventricular interdependence. NEET PG pitfall: Septal motion alone is not diagnostic; integrate Doppler, tissue Doppler, CT or MRI, and catheterization when needed.
management: Treat congestion cautiously and search for a reversible inflammatory phase. Persistent symptomatic constriction generally needs expert evaluation for pericardiectomy, while restrictive myocardial disease follows a different cause-specific pathway.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:10, watch for: The echocardiographic clip demonstrates respiratory interventricular dependence with a septal bounce. Core concept: Constrictive pericarditis limits total cardiac volume and exaggerates ventricular interdependence. Pitfall: Septal motion alone is not diagnostic; integrate Doppler, tissue Doppler, CT or MRI, and catheterization when needed.
anchors: Supportive Doppler findings include marked respiratory variation in mitral or tricuspid inflow and hepatic-vein expiratory diastolic reversal.
Annulus reversus describes medial e-prime exceeding lateral e-prime.
differentiation: A respiratory septal shift or bounce supports enhanced ventricular interdependence but is not specific by itself. Confirm with respirophasic mitral and tricuspid inflow changes, hepatic-vein flow, annular tissue Doppler, pericardial imaging, and invasive hemodynamics if the diagnosis remains unclear.

title: Wilson disease: wing-beating tremor
subject: Medicine / Neurology
source: 2.6. Wilson's Disease with Wing-Beating Tremor - Tremors [Spring Video Atlas] | Channel: Dr. Prodigious
summary: The short real-patient clip shows a coarse proximal tremor while both arms are held abducted and flexed in front of the chest.
identify: The proximal, coarse postural tremor emerges while the arms are abducted and flexed; its amplitude and rhythm require continuous observation.
examAngle: Wing-beating tremor is a proximal postural tremor associated with Wilson disease. NEET PG pitfall: A generic fine action tremor without the characteristic arm posture should not be called wing beating.
management: Use a copper chelator such as penicillamine or trientine for symptomatic copper overload and zinc in selected presymptomatic or maintenance settings. Monitor neurologic, hepatic, hematologic, renal, and treatment effects; acute liver failure or decompensated cirrhosis may require transplantation.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:14, watch for: The short real-patient clip shows a coarse proximal tremor while both arms are held abducted and flexed in front of the chest. Core concept: Wing-beating tremor is a proximal postural tremor associated with Wilson disease. Pitfall: A generic fine action tremor without the characteristic arm posture should not be called wing beating.
anchors: Wilson disease assessment includes slit-lamp examination, serum ceruloplasmin, urinary copper, and hepatic evaluation.
Neurologic Wilson disease may also produce dystonia, dysarthria, parkinsonism, or chorea.
differentiation: In a young patient with tremor, dystonia, dysarthria, psychiatric change, or liver disease, examine for Kayser-Fleischer rings and obtain ceruloplasmin, 24-hour urinary copper, liver tests, and appropriate genetic or hepatic evaluation. No single copper test should be interpreted in isolation.

title: Mitral regurgitation: systolic color-Doppler jet
subject: Medicine / Cardiology
source: Cardiac Color Mitral Regurgitation | Clinical Image | Channel: Butterfly Network
summary: The short cine loop shows a color-Doppler jet crossing the mitral valve from the left ventricle into the left atrium during systole.
identify: The regurgitant color jet appears during systole and changes frame by frame with leaflet motion, so cine playback is required.
examAngle: Mitral regurgitation produces systolic retrograde flow from the left ventricle to the left atrium. NEET PG pitfall: Do not grade mitral regurgitation from color-jet area alone, especially when the jet is eccentric.
management: Stabilize acute severe regurgitation and obtain urgent surgical assessment. Chronic primary severe disease is referred for repair before irreversible ventricular dysfunction; selected high-risk patients may receive transcatheter edge-to-edge repair, while secondary regurgitation also requires optimized heart-failure therapy.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:07, watch for: The short cine loop shows a color-Doppler jet crossing the mitral valve from the left ventricle into the left atrium during systole. Core concept: Mitral regurgitation produces systolic retrograde flow from the left ventricle to the left atrium. Pitfall: Do not grade mitral regurgitation from color-jet area alone, especially when the jet is eccentric.
anchors: Assess the jet in multiple views and integrate vena contracta, flow convergence, pulmonary-vein flow, and chamber response.
An eccentric wall-hugging jet can appear deceptively small because of the Coanda effect.
differentiation: Define the mechanism and severity of mitral regurgitation by transthoracic echo using an integrated assessment of valve anatomy, vena contracta, flow convergence, pulmonary-vein flow, ventricular and atrial response, and pulmonary pressure. Transesophageal echo is useful when anatomy or intervention planning is uncertain.

title: Lobar pneumonia: dynamic air bronchograms on lung ultrasound
subject: Medicine / Respiratory Medicine
source: 10-Pneumonia - Dynamic air & fluid bronchogram | Channel: NeoFOCUS-UK
summary: The lung-ultrasound loop shows bright air bronchograms moving centrifugally within tissue-like consolidated lung during inspiration.
identify: Centrifugal movement of air within bronchi during inspiration distinguishes a dynamic air bronchogram from a static one.
examAngle: Dynamic air bronchograms are hyperechoic air foci moving within bronchi during respiration. NEET PG pitfall: Static air bronchograms do not exclude pneumonia, but they are less specific than clearly dynamic bronchograms.
management: Give severity-appropriate antibiotics for suspected bacterial pneumonia, plus oxygen and supportive care when needed. Drain a significant complicated parapneumonic effusion or empyema and reassess when the clinical course or ultrasound pattern fails to improve.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:05, watch for: The lung-ultrasound loop shows bright air bronchograms moving centrifugally within tissue-like consolidated lung during inspiration. Core concept: Dynamic air bronchograms are hyperechoic air foci moving within bronchi during respiration. Pitfall: Static air bronchograms do not exclude pneumonia, but they are less specific than clearly dynamic bronchograms.
anchors: They strongly support pneumonia over obstructive resorptive atelectasis, where bronchograms are usually static.
Interpret them with the tissue-like lung pattern, shred sign, pleural appearance, and clinical context.
differentiation: Dynamic air bronchograms within tissue-like lung strongly favor pneumonia over obstructive atelectasis, but the diagnosis still uses fever, respiratory findings, oxygenation, laboratory data, and chest imaging. Pleural abnormalities, the shred sign, and focal B-lines strengthen the lung-ultrasound interpretation.

title: HOCM: systolic anterior motion on echocardiography
subject: Medicine / Cardiology
source: Systolic Anterior Motion of the Anterior Mitral Leaflet (SAM) | Channel: EchoDx Hub
summary: The echo loop shows the anterior mitral leaflet moving toward the hypertrophied septum during systole and narrowing the outflow tract.
identify: Systolic anterior motion is defined by the mitral leaflet moving toward the septum during systole, a phase-dependent cine finding.
examAngle: Systolic anterior motion can create dynamic left-ventricular outflow-tract obstruction in HOCM. NEET PG pitfall: Systolic anterior motion is not exclusive to genetic HOCM and can occur in other hyperdynamic, underfilled ventricles.
management: Avoid dehydration and drugs that markedly reduce preload in obstructive disease. A nonvasodilating beta blocker is usual first-line symptom treatment; refractory obstruction may require additional specialist therapy, septal reduction, and sudden-death risk assessment for an implantable defibrillator.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The echo loop shows the anterior mitral leaflet moving toward the hypertrophied septum during systole and narrowing the outflow tract. Core concept: Systolic anterior motion can create dynamic left-ventricular outflow-tract obstruction in HOCM. Pitfall: Systolic anterior motion is not exclusive to genetic HOCM and can occur in other hyperdynamic, underfilled ventricles.
anchors: Continuous-wave Doppler typically shows a late-peaking dagger-shaped velocity envelope when obstruction is present.
Reduced preload or afterload can increase obstruction, while increased filling and afterload usually reduce it.
differentiation: Echocardiography should document unexplained hypertrophy, systolic anterior motion, mitral regurgitation, and the resting and provoked LV outflow gradient. Obtain family history, ECG, ambulatory rhythm assessment, and genetic counseling or testing when hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is suspected.

title: Atrial fibrillation: absent a waves in the jugular venous pulse
subject: Medicine / Cardiology
source: Jugular Venous Pulse of Atrial Fibrillation | Channel: Andre Mansoor
summary: The real-patient close-up shows irregular jugular venous pulsations without a consistent presystolic a wave.
identify: Loss of organized a waves and irregular venous pulsations must be followed across several cardiac cycles in a real patient's neck.
examAngle: Atrial fibrillation abolishes coordinated right-atrial contraction, so organized JVP a waves disappear. NEET PG pitfall: Do not confuse an absent a wave with a merely difficult-to-see JVP caused by position, lighting, or low venous pressure.
management: Provide rate control or rhythm control according to symptoms, duration, comorbidity, and hemodynamic stability. Anticoagulation is based on validated stroke-risk assessment; unstable AF requires synchronized cardioversion, while reversible triggers and long-term risk factors are treated concurrently.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The real-patient close-up shows irregular jugular venous pulsations without a consistent presystolic a wave. Core concept: Atrial fibrillation abolishes coordinated right-atrial contraction, so organized JVP a waves disappear. Pitfall: Do not confuse an absent a wave with a merely difficult-to-see JVP caused by position, lighting, or low venous pressure.
anchors: The ventricular rhythm is irregularly irregular, and a pulse deficit may be present.
Confirm atrial fibrillation on ECG and assess thromboembolic and hemodynamic risk separately.
differentiation: Confirm atrial fibrillation on ECG by absent organized P waves and an irregularly irregular ventricular response. Evaluate triggers and structural disease, and estimate thromboembolic and bleeding risk rather than using the JVP finding alone.

title: Pulmonary embolism: McConnell sign on echocardiography
subject: Medicine / Cardiology
source: McConnell's sign | Channel: Mastering & Guidelines in Ultrasound & Echo-Card
summary: The 10-second apical echo loop shows right-ventricular free-wall akinesia with preserved apical contraction, the McConnell pattern.
identify: Right-ventricular free-wall akinesia with preserved apical motion must be recognized across several cardiac cycles.
examAngle: McConnell sign is regional right-ventricular dysfunction with mid-free-wall akinesia and apical sparing. NEET PG pitfall: Do not diagnose pulmonary embolism from McConnell sign alone; integrate probability, hemodynamics, and definitive imaging.
management: Start anticoagulation when pulmonary embolism is confirmed or strongly suspected without prohibitive bleeding risk. Hemodynamic collapse or persistent shock prompts reperfusion with systemic thrombolysis, catheter therapy, or surgical embolectomy according to contraindications and local expertise.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:10, watch for: The 10-second apical echo loop shows right-ventricular free-wall akinesia with preserved apical contraction, the McConnell pattern. Core concept: McConnell sign is regional right-ventricular dysfunction with mid-free-wall akinesia and apical sparing. Pitfall: Do not diagnose pulmonary embolism from McConnell sign alone; integrate probability, hemodynamics, and definitive imaging.
anchors: It supports acute right-ventricular strain but is not specific for pulmonary embolism and can occur with right-ventricular infarction.
In a hemodynamically stable patient with suspected pulmonary embolism, CT pulmonary angiography is the usual confirmatory test.
differentiation: Use clinical probability and D-dimer appropriately, with CT pulmonary angiography for most stable patients requiring definitive imaging. In shock, bedside echo showing right-heart strain can guide emergency decisions when transport is unsafe, but McConnell sign alone is not diagnostic.

title: High-quality CPR chest-compression sequence
subject: Medicine / Emergency Medicine
source: Chest Compressions (CPR Steps) | Channel: Singapore Civil Defence Force
summary: The uninterrupted mannequin sequence shows central hand placement, vertical compressions, full recoil, and minimal pauses.
identify: Rate, depth, recoil, hand position, and interruption time are performance variables that require continuous motion.
examAngle: Adult chest compressions should be delivered at 100 to 120 per minute to a depth of about 5 to 6 cm. NEET PG pitfall: Leaning on the chest between compressions reduces venous return even when the rate looks correct.
management: Begin high-quality chest compressions, provide ventilation, attach an AED or defibrillator, and follow the shockable or nonshockable arrest algorithm. Search for reversible causes and continue structured post-resuscitation care after return of spontaneous circulation.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The uninterrupted mannequin sequence shows central hand placement, vertical compressions, full recoil, and minimal pauses. Core concept: Adult chest compressions should be delivered at 100 to 120 per minute to a depth of about 5 to 6 cm. Pitfall: Leaning on the chest between compressions reduces venous return even when the rate looks correct.
anchors: Allow complete recoil and keep interruptions as short as possible.
Switch compressors about every two minutes when additional rescuers are available.
differentiation: Recognize cardiac arrest by unresponsiveness and absent normal breathing; a trained clinician checks a pulse for no more than 10 seconds. Monitor compression rate, depth, recoil, interruptions, and capnography when an advanced airway is present.

title: Grip myotonia in myotonic dystrophy
subject: Medicine / Neurology / Clinical Genetics
source: Handgrip myotonia | Channel: Neurology VIDEO
summary: After forcefully gripping the examiner's fingers, the real patient cannot immediately release the grip and the fingers relax slowly.
identify: Delayed opening after a forceful hand grip is a time-dependent failure of muscle relaxation and cannot be established from a posed hand image.
examAngle: Myotonia is delayed skeletal-muscle relaxation after voluntary contraction or percussion. NEET PG pitfall: Weak grip is not grip myotonia; the defining feature is delayed release after contraction.
management: Screen regularly for cardiac-conduction disease, respiratory weakness, sleep-disordered breathing, cataracts, and metabolic complications. Mexiletine can reduce disabling myotonia in selected patients; exercise, rehabilitation, genetic counseling, and special anesthesia precautions are essential.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: After forcefully gripping the examiner's fingers, the real patient cannot immediately release the grip and the fingers relax slowly. Core concept: Myotonia is delayed skeletal-muscle relaxation after voluntary contraction or percussion. Pitfall: Weak grip is not grip myotonia; the defining feature is delayed release after contraction.
anchors: Myotonic dystrophy type 1 is caused by a CTG-repeat expansion in DMPK and shows anticipation.
Look for distal weakness, facial wasting, frontal balding, cataracts, insulin resistance, and cardiac-conduction disease.
differentiation: Elicit grip and percussion myotonia, assess distal and facial weakness, and ask about cataracts, daytime somnolence, endocrine symptoms, and family history. EMG shows myotonic discharges, while molecular testing confirms the repeat-expansion disorder.

title: Motile microfilariae on a wet blood film
subject: Microbiology / Parasitology
source: Wuchereria bancrofti showing its microfilariae | Channel: Medical Lab Studies
summary: Slender microfilariae actively undulate among blood cells in the fresh wet-film field.
identify: The elongated larvae actively change shape and position among blood cells; motility is the feature that a still blood-film image cannot preserve.
examAngle: A wet film demonstrates living larval movement, while a stained thick or thin smear is needed for morphologic species identification. NEET PG pitfall: Do not identify a filarial species from motion alone; use sheath, nuclei, tail morphology, and epidemiology.
management: Give species- and region-appropriate antifilarial therapy through current public-health guidance and manage acute inflammatory episodes. Chronic lymphedema needs meticulous skin care, limb hygiene and exercise; hydrocele may require surgery.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: Slender microfilariae actively undulate among blood cells in the fresh wet-film field. Core concept: A wet film demonstrates living larval movement, while a stained thick or thin smear is needed for morphologic species identification. Pitfall: Do not identify a filarial species from motion alone; use sheath, nuclei, tail morphology, and epidemiology.
anchors: Wuchereria bancrofti and Brugia malayi usually show nocturnal periodicity, so collection time affects sensitivity.
Concentration methods or circulating-filarial-antigen testing can improve detection when the peripheral smear is negative.
differentiation: Collect blood at the species-appropriate periodic time and use thick and thin stained films, concentration methods, or antigen testing to confirm infection and identify the filaria. Evaluate lymphatic swelling, hydrocele, eosinophilia, renal involvement, and exposure history.

title: Trichomonas vaginalis motility on wet mount
subject: Microbiology / OBGYN
source: TRICHOMONAS : WET MOUNT SPECIMEN(FROM AIIMS MICROSCOPY LAB) | Channel: Shreyans Mutha
summary: The wet mount shows actively motile, flagellated Trichomonas vaginalis organisms.
identify: Jerky trophozoite motility is the discriminating feature and disappears in a still micrograph.
examAngle: Fresh saline microscopy reveals jerky or tumbling motility but has limited sensitivity. NEET PG pitfall: Loss of motility occurs quickly after collection, so a delayed negative wet mount is unreliable.
management: Treat the patient and current sexual partners with an effective nitroimidazole regimen, avoid intercourse until treatment is complete and symptoms resolve, and avoid alcohol as directed for the chosen drug. Retesting is recommended because reinfection is common.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The wet mount shows actively motile, flagellated Trichomonas vaginalis organisms. Core concept: Fresh saline microscopy reveals jerky or tumbling motility but has limited sensitivity. Pitfall: Loss of motility occurs quickly after collection, so a delayed negative wet mount is unreliable.
anchors: Trichomoniasis can cause frothy discharge, vulvovaginal irritation, and a strawberry cervix.
Treat the patient and sexual partners with a nitroimidazole and advise abstinence until treatment is complete.
differentiation: Motile flagellated trophozoites on a fresh wet mount are specific but insensitive; a nucleic-acid amplification test is preferred when available. Test for other sexually transmitted infections and remember that many infections are asymptomatic.

title: Treponema pallidum motility on dark-field microscopy
subject: Microbiology
source: Darkfield microsopy of Treponema pallidum syphilis spirochetes STI outpatient clinic GGD Amsterdam | Channel: Henry de Vries
summary: Live dark-field microscopy shows thin spirochetes flexing, rotating, and translating through the field.
identify: Flexion, rotation, and translational movement of thin spirochetes establish the live dark-field appearance.
examAngle: Treponema pallidum is too thin for reliable visualization with routine bright-field microscopy. NEET PG pitfall: A still spiral-shaped structure without characteristic motility is not enough to call Treponema pallidum.
management: Use intramuscular benzathine penicillin G for uncomplicated stage-appropriate disease and intravenous aqueous penicillin for neurosyphilis or ocular syphilis. Treat partners, test for HIV, follow quantitative nontreponemal titers, and anticipate a Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: Live dark-field microscopy shows thin spirochetes flexing, rotating, and translating through the field. Pitfall: A still spiral-shaped structure without characteristic motility is not enough to call Treponema pallidum.
anchors: Dark-field examination can support primary syphilis when motile organisms are seen in fresh lesion exudate.
Oral lesions are unsuitable because nonpathogenic oral spirochetes can create false identification.
differentiation: Confirm syphilis with a nontreponemal test for activity plus a treponemal test for specificity, or the reverse-sequence equivalent. Dark-field microscopy is useful only from suitable fresh lesions and does not replace staging or evaluation for neurologic, ocular, or congenital disease.

title: Vibrio cholerae: darting motility on hanging-drop microscopy
subject: Microbiology
source: Darting motility positive Vibrio cholerae in stool of cholera patient | Channel: Microhub Plus
summary: Numerous curved bacilli cross the hanging-drop field with rapid, directional darting movement.

examAngle: Vibrio cholerae is a curved, oxidase-positive, motile gram-negative bacillus with a polar flagellum. NEET PG pitfall: Brownian vibration lacks true directional travel and must not be mistaken for darting motility.
management: Rapid oral rehydration is the priority; severe dehydration requires prompt intravenous isotonic fluid followed by oral replacement. Give an effective local-resistance-guided antibiotic for severe cholera and zinc to children according to diarrhea guidance.

anchors: Specific antiserum immobilization can support presumptive identification of the darting organism.
Definitive laboratory work uses culture and biochemical or molecular identification; management priority is prompt fluid and electrolyte replacement.
differentiation: In acute profuse watery diarrhea, assess dehydration first and confirm outbreaks with stool culture, molecular testing, or validated rapid methods. Hanging-drop motility is a rapid demonstration but is not sufficient for final identification or antimicrobial susceptibility.

title: Giardia lamblia: falling-leaf motility on wet mount
subject: Microbiology / Parasitology

management: Treat with an effective antigiardial agent such as tinidazole, metronidazole, or nitazoxanide and restore hydration and nutrition. Persistent symptoms require checking adherence, reinfection, household transmission, immune deficiency, and post-infectious lactose intolerance.

Stool antigen or nucleic-acid testing is more sensitive than a single microscopy specimen; repeated samples may be required.
differentiation: Use stool antigen or nucleic-acid testing, or examine repeated stool specimens for cysts and trophozoites when giardiasis is suspected. Consider it in persistent greasy diarrhea, daycare or travel exposure, contaminated water, weight loss, or unexplained malabsorption.

title: Blood-culture venipuncture: needle insertion and initial blood draw
subject: Microbiology / Clinical Skills
source: Blood Culture Sample Collection | Blood Culture Collecting Techniques | Blood Culture Testing | Channel: Safercare
summary: The close-up begins as the blood-culture needle enters the prepared vein and shows the initial blood draw; bottle inoculation is outside this window.

management: Begin severity-appropriate therapy guided by local resistance, commonly ceftriaxone or azithromycin in many settings, then narrow to susceptibility results. Correct dehydration and complications, assess for intestinal bleeding or perforation, and address chronic carriage or public-health risk when relevant.
quickRevision: At 00:58-01:05, watch for: The close-up begins as the blood-culture needle enters the prepared vein and shows the initial blood draw; bottle inoculation is outside this window. anchors: Allow skin antiseptic to dry, avoid repalpating the prepared site, disinfect each culture-bottle septum, and collect the recommended number and volume of sets.
In suspected enteric fever, blood culture is most useful early in illness and permits antimicrobial-susceptibility testing; bone-marrow culture may remain positive after antibiotics.
differentiation: Obtain adequate-volume blood cultures before antibiotics when this does not delay care; yield is highest early in enteric fever and falls after antimicrobial exposure. Culture remains important for susceptibility, while bone-marrow culture can remain positive when blood culture is negative.

title: Listeria monocytogenes: tumbling motility
subject: Microbiology
source: Listeria monocytogenes tumbling motility | Channel: Diagnostic Bacteriology
summary: Short bacilli show a tumbling, end-over-end movement in the live microscopic preparation.
identify: Short rods repeatedly tumble and reorient in the wet preparation; this movement pattern, rather than morphology alone, is the tested clue.
examAngle: Listeria monocytogenes is a small gram-positive bacillus with tumbling motility, especially demonstrable near room temperature. NEET PG pitfall: Do not dismiss Listeria as a diphtheroid contaminant when isolated from blood or CSF in a compatible high-risk patient.
management: Use ampicillin or penicillin as directed therapy, often with gentamicin for selected severe infection; trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is a common alternative when beta-lactams cannot be used. Cephalosporins do not provide reliable Listeria coverage.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: Short bacilli show a tumbling, end-over-end movement in the live microscopic preparation. Pitfall: Do not dismiss Listeria as a diphtheroid contaminant when isolated from blood or CSF in a compatible high-risk patient.
anchors: It causes sepsis and meningitis in neonates, pregnant patients, older adults, and people with impaired cell-mediated immunity.
Listeria is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins; ampicillin is the classic directed therapy, often with gentamicin in severe disease.
differentiation: Confirm Listeria by blood or CSF culture and interpret it seriously in pregnancy, neonatal sepsis, meningitis, older age, or impaired cell-mediated immunity. CSF may resemble other bacterial meningitides and the organism can be mistaken for a contaminant.

title: Glass test for a non-blanching meningococcal rash
subject: Microbiology / Emergency Medicine
source: Learn how to diagnose signs of septic meningitis and other dangerous rashes. #glasstest | Channel: Dr. Abby
summary: A transparent glass is pressed against the rash to show that the lesions do not blanch.
identify: The relevant finding is persistence of the rash while transparent pressure is actively applied.
examAngle: Non-blanching petechiae or purpura indicate extravasated blood rather than simple vasodilation. NEET PG pitfall: A negative glass test does not exclude serious infection early in the illness.
management: Treat suspected meningococcal sepsis immediately with emergency assessment, parenteral antibiotics, oxygen, vascular access, and shock management without waiting for the rash or lumbar puncture. Notify public health and arrange indicated chemoprophylaxis and vaccination advice for close contacts.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: A transparent glass is pressed against the rash to show that the lesions do not blanch. Core concept: Non-blanching petechiae or purpura indicate extravasated blood rather than simple vasodilation. Pitfall: A negative glass test does not exclude serious infection early in the illness.
anchors: A rapidly progressive purpuric rash with fever and toxicity is a meningococcal emergency.
The glass test is a triage clue and does not identify the cause of purpura.
differentiation: A non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash with fever, toxicity, meningism, shock, or rapid progression is meningococcal disease until proved otherwise. The glass test is only a screening observation; normal early blanching does not exclude evolving sepsis.

title: Bacteriological water sampling: filling a sterile bottle from a tap
subject: Community Medicine
source: How To Collect A Drinking Water Total Coliform Sample | Channel: mnhealth
summary: The prepared tap stream is directed into the sterile bacteriological sampling bottle until the intended fill level is reached.
identify: The selected window shows the prepared tap stream entering the sterile bacteriological sampling bottle until the intended fill level is reached.

NEET PG pitfall: This window shows filling only; aseptic outlet preparation, bottle opening and closing, labeling, and transport must still be verified separately.
management: A contaminated result triggers repeat confirmation, source and distribution-system investigation, corrective disinfection or repair, and public-health risk communication. Advise boiling or an alternative safe supply when authorities judge consumption unsafe.

anchors: Remove outlet attachments, clean or disinfect the tap as required, and let water run before collecting without touching the rim or cap interior.
Label the site and collection time and transport the sample promptly under the prescribed cool conditions for bacteriological testing.
differentiation: Document site, outlet preparation, residual disinfectant, collection time, transport conditions, and laboratory method. Indicator organisms such as Escherichia coli are measured by validated membrane-filtration or multiple-tube methods; visual clarity does not establish microbiological safety.

title: Scapular winging during the wall-push test
subject: Anatomy / Neurology / Orthopedics
source: Winging of scapula wall push test | Channel: neurozone videos
summary: During a wall push, the medial border and inferior angle of one scapula lift prominently away from the thoracic wall.
identify: Medial-border prominence becomes evident and changes as the patient pushes against the wall, making the dynamic loading phase essential.
examAngle: Medial scapular winging during forward wall pressure indicates serratus-anterior weakness, usually from long-thoracic-nerve injury. NEET PG pitfall: Inspect the direction of winging and the provoking movement before assigning the involved muscle or nerve.
management: Treat the cause and preserve shoulder range with physiotherapy, scapular stabilization, and activity modification while nerve recovery is observed. Persistent disabling winging may need nerve repair, tendon transfer, or scapulothoracic stabilization in a specialist center.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: During a wall push, the medial border and inferior angle of one scapula lift prominently away from the thoracic wall. Core concept: Medial scapular winging during forward wall pressure indicates serratus-anterior weakness, usually from long-thoracic-nerve injury. Pitfall: Inspect the direction of winging and the provoking movement before assigning the involved muscle or nerve.
anchors: The long thoracic nerve arises mainly from C5-C7; injury may follow axillary surgery, traction, or repetitive overhead activity.
Trapezius weakness from spinal-accessory-nerve injury produces a different pattern that is accentuated by shoulder abduction.
differentiation: Compare winging during forward wall push, arm elevation, shrugging, and resisted movements, then examine serratus anterior, trapezius, rhomboids, and the cervical roots. EMG and nerve-conduction studies localize persistent weakness; imaging is selected for trauma, mass, or cervical disease.

title: Brodie-Trendelenburg test for venous reflux
subject: Surgery / Vascular
source: Examination of varicose veins ( Trendelenburgs test ) | Channel: MED PRO 100k
summary: The patient-positioning sequence demonstrates the Brodie-Trendelenburg test for superficial venous incompetence.
identify: The order of elevation, compression, standing, and release determines whether filling is superficial or perforator-mediated.
examAngle: Elevate the limb to empty superficial veins before applying an upper-thigh tourniquet. NEET PG pitfall: Modern duplex ultrasonography is the definitive reflux map; bedside tests are examination principles, not a replacement.
management: Use exercise, weight management, leg elevation, skin care, and compression when appropriate. Symptomatic truncal reflux or complications may be treated with endovenous ablation, ultrasound-guided sclerotherapy, or surgery according to duplex anatomy.
quickRevision: At 01:45-02:00, watch for: The patient-positioning sequence demonstrates the Brodie-Trendelenburg test for superficial venous incompetence. Core concept: Elevate the limb to empty superficial veins before applying an upper-thigh tourniquet. Pitfall: Modern duplex ultrasonography is the definitive reflux map; bedside tests are examination principles, not a replacement.
anchors: Rapid filling while the tourniquet remains on suggests incompetent perforators.
A sudden downward fill after tourniquet release suggests saphenofemoral junction incompetence.
differentiation: The Brodie-Trendelenburg test is a historical bedside clue to superficial venous reflux and cannot map the diseased segment reliably. Duplex ultrasound in the standing patient is the diagnostic standard for reflux, obstruction, vein diameter, and procedural planning.

title: Schwartz tap test for varicose-vein reflux

management: Manage symptoms and edema with mobility, elevation, skin care, and appropriate compression. Definitive treatment is anatomy-based and may use endovenous thermal or nonthermal ablation, sclerotherapy, phlebectomy, or surgery.

anchors: A transmitted impulse suggests an uninterrupted column with superficial valvular incompetence.
Duplex ultrasonography has replaced bedside tap tests for definitive reflux mapping.
differentiation: A transmitted tap suggests an uninterrupted superficial venous column, but the Schwartz test has limited accuracy. Confirm clinically important reflux and exclude deep venous obstruction with duplex ultrasound before intervention.

title: Acute appendicitis: noncompressible appendix on graded-compression ultrasound
subject: Surgery / Radiodiagnosis
source: Appendicitis ultrasound transverse with compression | Channel: JETem

management: Obtain early surgical review, analgesia, fluids, and antibiotics for complicated or strongly suspected disease. Appendectomy remains standard for many patients; carefully selected uncomplicated appendicitis may follow a local antibiotics-first protocol with recurrence counseling.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: With graded probe pressure, a blind-ending tubular structure remains round and does not collapse. A normal appendix should be compressible; nonvisualization alone neither confirms nor excludes appendicitis.
differentiation: Combine graded-compression ultrasound with migration of pain, right-lower-quadrant tenderness, fever, leukocytosis, and pregnancy status. If the appendix is not seen or findings conflict, repeat expert ultrasound, MRI, or CT is chosen according to age, pregnancy, and clinical risk.

title: Negative-pressure wound therapy: foam placement on a simulated wound
subject: Surgery
source: Basic Dressing Technique for Negative Pressure Wound Therapy | Channel: Tycon Express
summary: The selected simulator window shows wound foam being sized, placed into the wound cavity, and adjusted to fit without overlapping intact skin.

management: Debride devitalized tissue, protect vessels, organs, nerves, and fragile skin, and change dressings at the planned interval. Do not place foam directly over unprotected vital structures or untreated necrosis, and stop for bleeding, sepsis, ischemia, or device-related injury.
quickRevision: At 01:52-02:07, watch for: The selected simulator window shows wound foam being sized, placed into the wound cavity, and adjusted to fit without overlapping intact skin. Pitfall: This clip demonstrates foam placement only; it does not show drape application, connection to suction, or confirmation of a functioning seal.
anchors: Before application, assess perfusion, wound depth and undermining, infection, necrotic tissue, bleeding risk, and exposed vessels, organs, nerves, or anastomoses.
Count and document every foam piece, protect vulnerable structures, and confirm the prescribed pressure and an airtight seal after the complete dressing is assembled.
differentiation: Before negative-pressure therapy, define wound depth, undermining, perfusion, infection, exposed structures, and the need for debridement. An intact seal and prescribed pressure should produce controlled exudate removal without pain, bleeding, or tissue injury.

title: Flail chest: paradoxical chest-wall movement

management: Provide oxygen, multimodal or regional analgesia, pulmonary hygiene, and ventilatory support when gas exchange fails. Selected patients with severe displacement, respiratory compromise, or failure to wean benefit from surgical rib fixation.
quickRevision: At 00:20-00:35, watch for: The chest wall moves inward during inspiration and outward during expiration, demonstrating paradoxical motion. Core concept: A flail segment classically requires at least three adjacent ribs fractured in at least two places. Pitfall: Do not wait for dramatic paradoxical motion in a ventilated patient; positive pressure can mask the sign.
anchors: Associated pulmonary contusion often drives hypoxemia more than the unstable ribs themselves.
Analgesia, oxygenation, pulmonary toilet, and ventilatory support are tailored to respiratory status.
differentiation: Flail chest is a segment with at least three adjacent ribs fractured in two places, producing paradoxical motion; CT also defines pulmonary contusion and associated injury. Respiratory distress depends more on pain and underlying lung injury than on visible movement alone.

title: Infective endocarditis: mobile mitral-valve vegetation on echocardiography
subject: Medicine / Cardiology
source: Mitral valve endocarditis with rupture of the mitral valve | Channel: Heart and Lungs Ultrasound
summary: The echocardiographic loop shows an independently mobile mass attached to the mitral valve and oscillating with valve motion.
identify: An independently mobile mass oscillates with leaflet motion across consecutive cardiac cycles; cine behavior distinguishes it from a static valvular thickening.
examAngle: A mobile valvular vegetation is a major imaging feature of infective endocarditis in the appropriate clinical setting. NEET PG pitfall: Thrombus, Lambl excrescence, myxomatous tissue, and artifact can mimic a vegetation; echo must be integrated with microbiology and clinical findings.
management: Give prolonged intravenous, organism-directed bactericidal antibiotics with infectious-disease and cardiology input. Early valve surgery is considered for heart failure, uncontrolled infection, abscess or prosthetic complications, and selected high embolic-risk vegetations.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:13, watch for: The echocardiographic loop shows an independently mobile mass attached to the mitral valve and oscillating with valve motion. Core concept: A mobile valvular vegetation is a major imaging feature of infective endocarditis in the appropriate clinical setting. Pitfall: Thrombus, Lambl excrescence, myxomatous tissue, and artifact can mimic a vegetation; echo must be integrated with microbiology and clinical findings.
anchors: Obtain multiple blood-culture sets before antibiotics when the patient is stable enough, and apply the modified Duke framework.
Transesophageal echocardiography is more sensitive when transthoracic imaging is negative or prosthetic material and complications are suspected.
differentiation: Obtain at least three appropriately timed blood-culture sets and perform transthoracic echocardiography, followed by transesophageal imaging when sensitivity must be higher. Apply current Duke criteria and assess emboli, heart failure, abscess, conduction change, prosthetic material, and neurologic complications.

title: Surgeon's knot
subject: Surgery
source: Surgeon's Knot - The best technique | How to Hand Tie in 60 Seconds | Channel: Scrubbedout
summary: The hands form a surgeon's knot with an extra wrap on the first throw before alternating square throws.
identify: The extra wrap on the first throw and alternating direction are defined by hand sequence and tension.
examAngle: The double first throw increases friction and helps maintain tension while the second throw is placed. NEET PG pitfall: A double throw does not rescue poor square-knot technique or excessive tissue tension.
management: If a knot is loose, crossed, contaminated, or has damaged suture, replace it rather than adding arbitrary throws. Choose material and throw count for tissue, suture friction, and expected load, then cut tails to an appropriate length.
quickRevision: At 00:04-00:15, watch for: The hands form a surgeon's knot with an extra wrap on the first throw before alternating square throws. Core concept: The double first throw increases friction and helps maintain tension while the second throw is placed. Pitfall: A double throw does not rescue poor square-knot technique or excessive tissue tension.
anchors: Alternating hand direction is required to produce a flat square knot rather than a slipping granny knot.
Use the minimum number of secure throws appropriate for the suture material.
differentiation: A secure surgeon's knot has a friction-producing double first throw followed by flat alternating square throws. Inspect that it lies flat, does not slip under tension, and has not strangulated tissue; a granny knot has repeated same-direction throws and is unreliable.

title: BCG vaccination: intradermal injection with wheal formation
subject: Community Medicine / Pediatrics
source: BCG vaccination | Channel: HSE Ireland
summary: A fine needle enters the skin almost parallel to the surface and a small raised intradermal wheal appears during BCG injection.
identify: Correct intradermal placement is demonstrated by the shallow needle angle and immediate raised wheal while vaccine is injected.
examAngle: BCG is a live attenuated Mycobacterium bovis vaccine given intradermally, usually over the left upper arm in the Indian program. NEET PG pitfall: A deep subcutaneous injection that produces no wheal is incorrect technique and increases local adverse reactions.
management: Do not routinely revaccinate solely because a scar is absent. Explain the expected local evolution and assess persistent ulceration, suppurative lymphadenitis, osteitis, or disseminated disease, especially when immunodeficiency is possible.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: A fine needle enters the skin almost parallel to the surface and a small raised intradermal wheal appears during BCG injection. Core concept: BCG is a live attenuated Mycobacterium bovis vaccine given intradermally, usually over the left upper arm in the Indian program. Pitfall: A deep subcutaneous injection that produces no wheal is incorrect technique and increases local adverse reactions.
anchors: Correct placement produces an immediate pale wheal; a local papule, ulcer, and scar subsequently develop.
Its main childhood benefit is protection against severe disseminated tuberculosis and tuberculous meningitis.
differentiation: Correct BCG technique is confirmed by a superficial intradermal wheal at injection, not by aspiration or a later scar. Record the administered dose and site and screen for contraindications to a live vaccine, including severe cellular immunodeficiency.

title: Proximal-tibial intraosseous needle insertion on a pediatric simulator
subject: Surgery / Emergency Medicine
source: Emergency Procedures: Intraosseous Access - EZ-IO | Channel: UNMC HEROES
summary: On a pediatric simulator, the powered needle is placed at the proximal-tibial landmark, advanced into bone, and stabilized.
identify: The selected window shows only landmark alignment, powered insertion, loss of resistance, and needle stabilization rather than device explanation.
examAngle: The proximal tibia is a common emergency intraosseous site; avoid the physis, a fractured bone, local infection, and a recently attempted site. NEET PG pitfall: Pain, resistance, or swelling during infusion suggests extravasation and possible compartment syndrome.
management: Use the line immediately for resuscitation after secure placement, with pressure-assisted infusion and analgesia for conscious patients. Replace it with reliable intravenous or central access as soon as practical and remove promptly if position or tissue safety is uncertain.
quickRevision: At 03:07-03:14, watch for: On a pediatric simulator, the powered needle is placed at the proximal-tibial landmark, advanced into bone, and stabilized. Core concept: The proximal tibia is a common emergency intraosseous site; avoid the physis, a fractured bone, local infection, and a recently attempted site. Pitfall: Pain, resistance, or swelling during infusion suggests extravasation and possible compartment syndrome.
anchors: A firm loss of resistance, stable needle, easy flush, and no soft-tissue swelling support correct placement.
Resuscitation fluids, blood products, and most emergency drugs can be delivered intraosseously until intravenous access is secured.
differentiation: Confirm placement by needle stability, appropriate depth marking, aspiration when obtainable, and an easy saline flush without swelling; absence of marrow return does not by itself prove failure. Monitor the limb continuously for extravasation and compartment syndrome.

title: Triangle-of-safety landmarking before chest-drain insertion
subject: Surgery / Trauma
source: Chest Drain Insertion on Simulated Patient | Professor Stuart Enoch | Channel: Doctors Academy
summary: The short window identifies the triangle of safety on the lateral chest before any incision or tube insertion begins.
identify: The segment demonstrates the moving hand landmarks that define the triangle of safety; it does not claim to show pleural entry or tube placement.
examAngle: Its borders are the lateral edge of pectoralis major, anterior edge of latissimus dorsi, a line above the fifth intercostal space, and the axillary apex. NEET PG pitfall: The clip demonstrates landmarking only; it must not be interpreted as showing pleural entry or completed drain placement.
management: After landmarking, provide asepsis and analgesia, dissect over the rib, confirm pleural entry with a finger, position all side holes intrathoracically, connect drainage, and secure the tube. Reassess breathing and obtain appropriate post-procedure imaging.
quickRevision: At 00:31-00:39, watch for: The short window identifies the triangle of safety on the lateral chest before any incision or tube insertion begins. Core concept: Its borders are the lateral edge of pectoralis major, anterior edge of latissimus dorsi, a line above the fifth intercostal space, and the axillary apex. Pitfall: The clip demonstrates landmarking only; it must not be interpreted as showing pleural entry or completed drain placement.
anchors: The usual entry is in the fourth or fifth intercostal space just anterior to the midaxillary line, adapted to the indication and anatomy.
The tract is made over the upper border of a rib to reduce injury to the main intercostal neurovascular bundle.
differentiation: Choose the side and interspace from the indication, examination, and imaging, and use ultrasound for pleural fluid when feasible. Mark the triangle of safety with the patient positioned for access; avoid a site over infection, a low subdiaphragmatic entry, or an unverified landmark.

title: Adult choking: abdominal-thrust hand placement and thrusts
subject: Surgery / Emergency Medicine
source: How to do the Heimlich maneuver | Channel: ParkviewHealth
summary: The rescuer places a fist above the umbilicus and below the xiphoid, then gives repeated inward-and-upward abdominal thrusts.
identify: The clip begins at rescuer position and shows fist placement plus repeated inward-and-upward thrusts without the introductory title.
examAngle: Abdominal thrusts are used for a conscious adult with severe foreign-body airway obstruction who cannot cough or speak effectively. NEET PG pitfall: Never place the fist on the xiphoid and never perform a blind finger sweep.
management: For a conscious adult with severe obstruction, use alternating cycles of 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts, starting with back blows; use chest thrusts when abdominal thrusts are unsuitable. If consciousness is lost, activate emergency response, begin CPR, and remove only a clearly visible object.
quickRevision: At 00:25-00:40, watch for: The rescuer places a fist above the umbilicus and below the xiphoid, then gives repeated inward-and-upward abdominal thrusts. Core concept: Abdominal thrusts are used for a conscious adult with severe foreign-body airway obstruction who cannot cough or speak effectively. Pitfall: Never place the fist on the xiphoid and never perform a blind finger sweep.
anchors: Use chest thrusts in late pregnancy or when the abdomen cannot be encircled.
If the patient becomes unresponsive, lower the patient safely, activate emergency response, and begin CPR.
differentiation: Severe obstruction is recognized by ineffective cough, inability to speak or breathe, cyanosis, or the universal choking sign. A person who coughs effectively should be encouraged and observed rather than immediately receiving forceful thrusts.

title: Cord prolapse: manual elevation of the presenting part on a simulator
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
source: Umbilical Cord Prolapse | Channel: TLC Medical Education
summary: In simulation, a gloved hand elevates the presenting part away from the prolapsed cord to relieve compression.
identify: A gloved hand is inserted beside the visibly prolapsed cord and maintained in position to elevate the presenting part on an obstetric simulator.
examAngle: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency because compression can cause acute fetal hypoxia. NEET PG pitfall: Do not attempt to replace the exposed cord into the uterus or release elevation before definitive delivery is ready.
management: Call for help, manually elevate the presenting part, position the mother to reduce compression, minimize cord exposure, and expedite birth, usually by emergency cesarean section. Continue elevation until delivery or an effective alternative decompression is maintained.
quickRevision: At 00:20-00:35, watch for: In simulation, a gloved hand elevates the presenting part away from the prolapsed cord to relieve compression. Core concept: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency because compression can cause acute fetal hypoxia. Pitfall: Do not attempt to replace the exposed cord into the uterus or release elevation before definitive delivery is ready.
anchors: Maintain manual elevation, minimize cord handling, position the mother to reduce pressure, and prepare immediate delivery, usually emergency cesarean birth.
Bladder filling can maintain elevation during transfer when manual support is difficult; fetal monitoring and resuscitation proceed simultaneously.
differentiation: Suspect cord prolapse after membrane rupture when fetal bradycardia or recurrent variable decelerations occur; confirm by seeing or gently palpating cord below or alongside the presenting part. Avoid repeated handling and assess whether immediate vaginal birth is truly faster and safe.

title: Pinard maneuver: delivery of an extended fetal leg on a simulator
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
source: MamaBirthie - Breech Delivery | Channel: Laerdal Global Health
summary: On a breech-delivery simulator, the clinician flexes an extended fetal knee and sweeps the lower leg and foot downward for delivery.
identify: The close-up shows the extended fetal leg being flexed at the knee and swept downward during simulated assisted breech delivery.
examAngle: The Pinard maneuver is used when a fetal leg remains extended during assisted vaginal breech delivery. NEET PG pitfall: Do not pull directly on an extended leg or fetal trunk; identify the knee and flex it before sweeping the foot downward.
management: Use a coordinated breech sequence with minimal traction, continuous fetal assessment, and timely maneuvers for extended arms or the after-coming head. If descent fails, fetal compromise develops, or safe vaginal criteria are not met, proceed promptly to the appropriate operative delivery rather than repeating forceful manipulation.
quickRevision: At 01:53-02:08, watch for: On a breech-delivery simulator, the clinician flexes an extended fetal knee and sweeps the lower leg and foot downward for delivery. Core concept: The Pinard maneuver is used when a fetal leg remains extended during assisted vaginal breech delivery. Pitfall: Do not pull directly on an extended leg or fetal trunk; identify the knee and flex it before sweeping the foot downward.
anchors: Two fingers press in the popliteal fossa to flex the knee while the leg is abducted and swept down without forceful traction on the foot.
Allow spontaneous delivery to the umbilicus when possible, keep the fetal back anterior, and use a planned sequence for extended arms or the after-coming head if required.
differentiation: Before assisted vaginal breech birth, confirm presentation, fetal attitude and estimated size, pelvic adequacy, cervical dilatation, fetal wellbeing, and immediate access to operative delivery and neonatal resuscitation. Pinard is recognized by pressure in the popliteal fossa to flex an extended knee, followed by abduction and downward sweeping of the leg.

title: Intrauterine balloon tamponade for atonic PPH
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
source: PPH: Uterine Tamponade | Channel: Obstetrics Gynecology and Life
summary: The animation shows insertion, staged filling, drainage observation, and securing of an intrauterine balloon for atonic postpartum hemorrhage.
identify: Insertion, uterine positioning, staged filling, drainage observation, and securing the catheter are procedural.
examAngle: Balloon tamponade is considered after uterotonics and basic measures fail in ongoing atonic postpartum hemorrhage. NEET PG pitfall: Do not insert a balloon before evaluating retained tissue, genital-tract trauma, uterine rupture, and coagulopathy.
management: Continue uterine massage, uterotonics, tranexamic acid when indicated, transfusion and correction of coagulopathy while the balloon is placed. Failure to control hemorrhage demands immediate escalation to compression sutures, devascularization, embolization, or hysterectomy.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The animation shows insertion, staged filling, drainage observation, and securing of an intrauterine balloon for atonic postpartum hemorrhage. Core concept: Balloon tamponade is considered after uterotonics and basic measures fail in ongoing atonic postpartum hemorrhage. Pitfall: Do not insert a balloon before evaluating retained tissue, genital-tract trauma, uterine rupture, and coagulopathy.
anchors: Fill with sterile fluid while monitoring bleeding through the drainage channel and vaginally.
Continue resuscitation and prepare escalation if the tamponade test fails.
differentiation: Use balloon tamponade for ongoing uterine bleeding after rapid assessment for tone, retained tissue, trauma, rupture, and coagulopathy. A fall in bleeding through and around the catheter after filling is a positive tamponade response, but vitals and blood loss still require close monitoring.

title: Woods corkscrew maneuver for shoulder dystocia
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
source: Shoulder Dystocia Woods Screw Maneuver Animation by Cal Shipley, M.D. | Channel: Dr. Cal Shipley, M.D.
summary: An internal hand applies pressure to the posterior shoulder and rotates the fetal shoulders through a corkscrew arc.
identify: The fetal shoulders are rotated through coordinated pressure on the posterior surface; hand position and direction are decisive.
examAngle: The Woods maneuver rotates the shoulders rather than applying traction to the fetal head. NEET PG pitfall: Fundal pressure worsens shoulder impaction and must not be used.
management: Use a rehearsed sequence beginning with McRoberts positioning and suprapubic pressure, then posterior-arm delivery or internal rotation such as Woods when needed. Prepare neonatal resuscitation, assess maternal trauma, and document timing and maneuvers after delivery.
quickRevision: At 00:05-00:20, watch for: An internal hand applies pressure to the posterior shoulder and rotates the fetal shoulders through a corkscrew arc. Core concept: The Woods maneuver rotates the shoulders rather than applying traction to the fetal head. Pitfall: Fundal pressure worsens shoulder impaction and must not be used.
anchors: Identify fetal orientation before choosing the hand and direction of pressure.
Use it within a timed shoulder-dystocia sequence alongside McRoberts positioning, suprapubic pressure, posterior-arm delivery, or other indicated maneuvers.
differentiation: Shoulder dystocia is diagnosed when the shoulders fail to deliver with gentle axial traction after the head, often with retraction of the head against the perineum. Announce the emergency, note the time, stop pushing and fundal pressure, and determine fetal orientation before rotational maneuvers.

title: Rubin I maneuver: suprapubic pressure to adduct the anterior shoulder
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
source: Rubins Maneuver | Channel: Dr_Max_Mongelli
summary: External suprapubic pressure is directed obliquely behind the anterior fetal shoulder to adduct it and reduce the shoulder diameter.
identify: The animation visibly applies lateral suprapubic pressure toward the fetal chest; the title no longer overstates this as an internal Rubin II maneuver.
examAngle: Rubin I is an external maneuver and is commonly attempted with McRoberts positioning. NEET PG pitfall: Do not confuse external Rubin I suprapubic pressure with internal Rubin II pressure on the accessible shoulder.
management: Apply continuous or rocking suprapubic pressure while gentle axial traction is attempted; move promptly to posterior-arm delivery or an internal rotational maneuver if unsuccessful. Avoid forceful downward head traction and record the sequence and duration.
quickRevision: At 00:23-00:32, watch for: External suprapubic pressure is directed obliquely behind the anterior fetal shoulder to adduct it and reduce the shoulder diameter. Core concept: Rubin I is an external maneuver and is commonly attempted with McRoberts positioning. Pitfall: Do not confuse external Rubin I suprapubic pressure with internal Rubin II pressure on the accessible shoulder.
anchors: Pressure is applied from the side of the fetal back toward the fetal chest, not straight downward without regard to orientation.
The aim is shoulder adduction and rotation into an oblique pelvic diameter while excessive head traction is avoided.
differentiation: Rubin I is identified by external oblique suprapubic pressure directed behind the anterior shoulder, usually during McRoberts positioning. Confirm that pressure is suprapubic rather than fundal and is aimed according to the fetal back.

title: Rooting reflex in a newborn
subject: Pediatrics / Neonatology
source: Primitive reflexes: rooting reflex | Channel: Dr. Prodigious
summary: Stroking the newborn's cheek causes the head to turn toward the stimulus and the mouth to open in a feeding search.
identify: Stroking the cheek produces a time-linked head turn and mouth opening toward the stimulus; stimulus and response must be seen together.
examAngle: The rooting reflex helps a newborn locate the nipple and depends on intact brainstem and cranial-nerve function. NEET PG pitfall: Touch the cheek near the mouth; lip movement alone without head turning is an incomplete response.
management: An isolated weak response in a sleepy newborn may only need repeat examination. Persistent absence, asymmetry, poor feeding, or other neurologic signs requires airway and feeding safety assessment, lactation support, and pediatric or neurologic evaluation.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:08, watch for: Stroking the newborn's cheek causes the head to turn toward the stimulus and the mouth to open in a feeding search. Core concept: The rooting reflex helps a newborn locate the nipple and depends on intact brainstem and cranial-nerve function. Pitfall: Touch the cheek near the mouth; lip movement alone without head turning is an incomplete response.
anchors: It is strongest when the infant is hungry and awake and normally fades by roughly three to four months.
Absence may occur with prematurity, depression, or neurologic dysfunction; persistence suggests impaired cortical maturation.
differentiation: Assess rooting with the infant awake and calm, comparing sides and integrating suck, latch, facial movement, tone, alertness, and other primitive reflexes. Gestational age, recent feeding, sedating medication, and illness can alter the response.

title: Lovset maneuver for extended fetal arms
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
source: Lovset's manoeuvre | Assisted breech delivery | Medicine | Obstetrics | Channel: Madicine medic
summary: The obstetric simulator shows rotational delivery of extended arms using the Lovset maneuver.
identify: Sequential trunk rotation brings each posterior shoulder anterior and sweeps each arm across the chest.
examAngle: Hold the fetal pelvis and rotate the trunk so the posterior shoulder becomes anterior beneath the symphysis. NEET PG pitfall: Forceful traction on the humerus risks fracture; the rotation should mobilize the shoulder girdle instead.
management: Rotate the body to bring the posterior shoulder anterior under the symphysis, sweep the arm across the chest, then reverse the rotation for the other arm. Experienced obstetric assistance, neonatal resuscitation readiness, and avoidance of excessive traction are essential.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The obstetric simulator shows rotational delivery of extended arms using the Lovset maneuver. Core concept: Hold the fetal pelvis and rotate the trunk so the posterior shoulder becomes anterior beneath the symphysis. Pitfall: Forceful traction on the humerus risks fracture; the rotation should mobilize the shoulder girdle instead.
anchors: Deliver the arm by sweeping it across the chest, then rotate in the opposite direction for the other arm.
The maneuver is used for extended or nuchal arms during assisted vaginal breech birth.
differentiation: Lovset maneuver is used during assisted breech birth when the fetal arms are extended or nuchal and do not deliver spontaneously. Confirm sacral orientation and rotate the fetal trunk while supporting the bony pelvis rather than pulling on soft tissues.

title: Controlled cord traction
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
source: Control Cord Traction Procedure ( Placenta separation) #simucon #sbims #placenta separation #mbbs | Channel: Tushar Sinha MBBS
summary: The clip demonstrates controlled cord traction with uterine support during placental delivery.
identify: Counter-traction, uterine contraction, cord traction, and placental delivery must be synchronized.
