examAngle: Controlled cord traction is performed after uterotonic administration and signs of placental separation when a skilled attendant is present. NEET PG pitfall: Cord traction without uterine counterpressure can contribute to uterine inversion.
management: Wait for a firm contracted uterus, apply steady traction in the birth-canal axis, and stop if the placenta does not descend. Manage retained placenta or hemorrhage according to protocol rather than repeatedly pulling on the cord.
quickRevision: At 01:42-01:57, watch for: The clip demonstrates controlled cord traction with uterine support during placental delivery. Core concept: Controlled cord traction is performed after uterotonic administration and signs of placental separation when a skilled attendant is present. Pitfall: Cord traction without uterine counterpressure can contribute to uterine inversion.
anchors: One hand provides suprapubic countertraction while the other applies steady traction along the birth canal.
After delivery, inspect the placenta and membranes for completeness and assess uterine tone.
differentiation: Controlled cord traction is performed only after a uterotonic and signs of placental separation, with one hand providing suprapubic uterine countertraction. A noncontracted uterus, absent countertraction, or forceful traction raises the risk of inversion and cord avulsion.

title: Vacuum cup flexion-point measurement on a simulator
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
source: Best Practice Measuring to Place Cup on Flexion Point | Channel: STABLEProgram
summary: On a simulator, the distance along the sagittal suture is measured to locate the vacuum cup flexion point before application.
identify: The selected window measures the distance to the flexion point on the cup; it does not claim to show cup application to the fetal scalp.
examAngle: The flexion point lies on the sagittal suture about 3 cm anterior to the posterior fontanelle. NEET PG pitfall: The linked segment shows measurement only; it does not demonstrate cup traction or a completed vacuum delivery.
management: Apply traction only with contractions and maternal effort, in the pelvic-axis direction, while monitoring descent and cup detachments. Abandon the attempt when progress or safety limits are not met and examine the neonate for scalp and intracranial complications.
quickRevision: At 00:53-01:04, watch for: On a simulator, the distance along the sagittal suture is measured to locate the vacuum cup flexion point before application. Core concept: The flexion point lies on the sagittal suture about 3 cm anterior to the posterior fontanelle. Pitfall: The linked segment shows measurement only; it does not demonstrate cup traction or a completed vacuum delivery.
anchors: Correct placement promotes flexion and presents a smaller fetal head diameter through the pelvis.
Before vacuum delivery, confirm full cervical dilatation, ruptured membranes, engaged head, known position, and absence of contraindications.
differentiation: Before vacuum use, confirm vertex presentation, full dilation, ruptured membranes, engaged head, exact fetal position, adequate pelvis, analgesia, and an empty bladder. The cup is centered over the flexion point, with no maternal tissue trapped beneath its rim.

title: POP-Q ruler measurement from the hymenal reference point on a pelvic model
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
source: POP- Q Part 2- Practical Demonstration @Love_Obs_Gynae | Channel: Love Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary: A marked ruler measures prolapse points in centimeters above or below the hymenal plane on a pelvic model.
identify: The 15-second window begins when the ruler is placed and a compartment point is measured relative to the hymen, avoiding the preceding lecture.
examAngle: The hymen is the fixed zero reference: points above it are negative and points beyond it are positive. NEET PG pitfall: Do not report distances from the introitus or instrument tip; POP-Q measurements are referenced to the hymen.
management: Offer observation or pelvic-floor therapy for mild symptoms, a fitted pessary for conservative support, and compartment-specific reconstructive or obliterative surgery when indicated. Management should reflect symptoms, fertility and sexual goals, comorbidity, and recurrence risk.
quickRevision: At 05:44-05:50, watch for: A marked ruler measures prolapse points in centimeters above or below the hymenal plane on a pelvic model. Core concept: The hymen is the fixed zero reference: points above it are negative and points beyond it are positive. Pitfall: Do not report distances from the introitus or instrument tip; POP-Q measurements are referenced to the hymen.
anchors: POP-Q records Aa, Ba, C, D, Ap, Bp, genital hiatus, perineal body, and total vaginal length.
Measurements should be taken during maximal strain with the compartment being assessed fully exposed.
differentiation: Perform all nine POP-Q measurements during maximal strain and assign stage from the most distal point relative to the hymen. Also assess urinary, bowel, sexual, uterine, apical, and pelvic-floor symptoms because stage and symptom burden do not always match.

title: Stepping reflex in a newborn
subject: Pediatrics / Neonatology
source: Primitive reflexes: stepping reflex | Channel: Dr. Prodigious
summary: Held upright with the soles touching a firm surface, the newborn makes alternating step-like leg movements.
identify: With the infant held upright and the soles touching a surface, alternating step-like leg movements appear over several seconds.
examAngle: The stepping reflex is a primitive spinal and brainstem motor pattern present at birth. NEET PG pitfall: Supported stepping is not evidence that a newborn has attained the developmental milestone of independent walking.
management: No treatment is required for a symmetric age-appropriate reflex. Persistent asymmetry, absent leg movement, abnormal tone, or broader developmental concern warrants repeat assessment and early pediatric, orthopedic, or neurologic referral.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: Held upright with the soles touching a firm surface, the newborn makes alternating step-like leg movements. Core concept: The stepping reflex is a primitive spinal and brainstem motor pattern present at birth. Pitfall: Supported stepping is not evidence that a newborn has attained the developmental milestone of independent walking.
anchors: It usually becomes difficult to elicit by about two months as leg mass increases, before voluntary walking later develops.
Assess symmetry; a consistently absent or asymmetric response should be interpreted with tone, strength, hip stability, and the rest of the neurologic examination.
differentiation: Elicit stepping with the infant held upright, head and trunk supported, and soles contacting a firm surface. Interpret asymmetry with hip examination, leg movement, tone, primitive reflexes, and gestational age rather than using it as a stand-alone developmental test.

title: External cephalic version for transverse lie
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
source: EXTERNAL CEPHALIC VERSION for BREECH BABY. An option for achieving a safe vaginal delivery. | Channel: OBSTETEAM
summary: The real-patient clip shows ultrasound-guided hand placement and controlled external rotation of the fetus.
identify: Hand placement, disimpaction, forward roll, fetal movement, and monitoring are continuous actions.
examAngle: External cephalic version is generally offered near term for an uncomplicated non-cephalic presentation. NEET PG pitfall: Do not attempt ECV when vaginal birth is contraindicated, such as with placenta previa.
management: Use informed consent, appropriate analgesia and usually tocolysis, then monitor fetal status before and after the attempt. Give anti-D to an unsensitized Rh-negative patient when indicated and plan mode of birth according to the final presentation.
quickRevision: At 00:10-00:25, watch for: The real-patient clip shows ultrasound-guided hand placement and controlled external rotation of the fetus. Core concept: External cephalic version is generally offered near term for an uncomplicated non-cephalic presentation. Pitfall: Do not attempt ECV when vaginal birth is contraindicated, such as with placenta previa.
anchors: Confirm fetal position, placental location, fluid, and fetal heart status before and after the attempt.
Tocolysis can improve success; give anti-D prophylaxis to an unsensitized Rh-negative patient when indicated.
differentiation: Before external cephalic version, use ultrasound to confirm lie, placental position, fluid, and absence of a contraindication, and document a reassuring fetal heart pattern. The procedure is performed where urgent cesarean birth can be provided.

title: Galant reflex in a newborn
subject: Pediatrics / Neonatology
source: Newborn normal primitive reflexes: Galant | Channel: onlinemedicalvideo
summary: Stroking one side of the paraspinal skin makes the newborn's trunk curve toward the stimulated side.
identify: Paraspinal stroking produces ipsilateral trunk incurvation; the directional response immediately after stimulation is the tested feature.
examAngle: The Galant reflex is a primitive truncal-incurvation response mediated at spinal and brainstem levels. NEET PG pitfall: Pelvic movement caused by an unstable hold is not a true Galant response; look for reproducible lateral trunk flexion.
management: A symmetric age-appropriate response needs no treatment. Persistent asymmetry, weakness, abnormal tone, or delayed motor development calls for pediatric neurologic assessment and early physiotherapy or developmental intervention.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: Stroking one side of the paraspinal skin makes the newborn's trunk curve toward the stimulated side. Core concept: The Galant reflex is a primitive truncal-incurvation response mediated at spinal and brainstem levels. Pitfall: Pelvic movement caused by an unstable hold is not a true Galant response; look for reproducible lateral trunk flexion.
anchors: It is present at birth and normally integrates during early infancy, commonly by about two to four months.
Marked asymmetry or persistence should prompt assessment for peripheral, spinal, or central neurologic dysfunction.
differentiation: Elicit the Galant response in prone or ventral suspension by stroking one paraspinal side from shoulder toward buttock. Compare both sides and interpret it with spinal alignment, limb movement, tone, anal reflexes, and the rest of the newborn neurologic examination.

title: Unresponsive choking child: airway opening and visible-object check
subject: Pediatrics / Emergency Medicine
source: Unconscious Child Choking - Lay Rescuer | Channel: ProCPR
summary: The rescuer opens the unresponsive child's airway and checks the mouth for a clearly visible foreign body before the next CPR cycle.
identify: The selected window shows the rescuer opening the airway and checking for a clearly visible object; it does not claim to show the compression phase.
examAngle: Use 30:2 for a single rescuer and 15:2 for two trained rescuers performing pediatric CPR. NEET PG pitfall: Do not perform blind finger sweeps in an unresponsive child.
management: Use 30 compressions to 2 breaths for one rescuer and 15 to 2 for two trained rescuers, attaching an AED as soon as available. Continue cycles, reassess rhythm and breathing, and arrange medical evaluation after object removal because residual airway injury or material may remain.
quickRevision: At 01:24-01:35, watch for: The rescuer opens the unresponsive child's airway and checks the mouth for a clearly visible foreign body before the next CPR cycle. Core concept: Use 30:2 for a single rescuer and 15:2 for two trained rescuers performing pediatric CPR. Pitfall: Do not perform blind finger sweeps in an unresponsive child.
anchors: After each compression cycle, open the airway and remove an object only if it is clearly visible.
Activate emergency response and obtain an AED as soon as possible.
differentiation: When a choking child becomes unresponsive, confirm absent normal breathing, activate emergency response, and begin CPR rather than continuing standing thrusts. Each time the airway is opened, remove an object only if it is clearly visible.

title: Intussusception on real-time ultrasound
subject: Pediatrics / Radiodiagnosis
source: intussusception ultrasound video | Channel: Ultrasound videos
summary: The real-time ultrasound sweep shows a persistent concentric bowel-within-bowel configuration changing between transverse and longitudinal views.
identify: The concentric bowel-within-bowel structure changes through a real-time sweep and must be confirmed dynamically in more than one plane.
examAngle: Intussusception commonly appears as a target or doughnut sign transversely and a pseudokidney appearance longitudinally. NEET PG pitfall: A transient small-bowel intussusception can resolve spontaneously; correlate persistence, size, symptoms, and location.
management: Resuscitate with fluids, analgesia, and surgical consultation. A stable ileocolic intussusception without perforation is usually reduced by image-guided air or hydrostatic enema; failed reduction, perforation, peritonitis, or a pathologic lead point requires surgery.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The real-time ultrasound sweep shows a persistent concentric bowel-within-bowel configuration changing between transverse and longitudinal views. Core concept: Intussusception commonly appears as a target or doughnut sign transversely and a pseudokidney appearance longitudinally. Pitfall: A transient small-bowel intussusception can resolve spontaneously; correlate persistence, size, symptoms, and location.
anchors: Ileocolic intussusception is the commonest form in young children.
A stable child without perforation or peritonitis is usually treated with image-guided pneumatic or hydrostatic reduction.
differentiation: Ultrasound confirms a persistent bowel-within-bowel target in transverse view and pseudokidney appearance longitudinally, while also looking for lead point, free fluid, perfusion, and trapped fluid. Peritonitis, shock, or free air signals possible ischemia or perforation.

title: SSPE: myoclonic jerks
subject: Pediatrics / Neurology
source: Myoclonus Jerks videos of two patients. (case of SSPE), case detail given in comment. | Channel: DR BILAL Khanzada (Consultant Neurologist)
summary: The real patient has repeated brief myoclonic jerks with a strikingly periodic pattern.
identify: Brief recurrent jerks and their stereotyped periodicity are movement findings that a photograph cannot convey.
examAngle: Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis is a delayed progressive complication of measles infection. NEET PG pitfall: Periodic SSPE myoclonus should not be dismissed as random behavioral movement or isolated epilepsy.
management: There is no reliably curative therapy; neurologic and palliative care addresses seizures, spasticity, nutrition, aspiration, mobility, and family support. Antiviral or immunomodulatory regimens have variable benefit, while timely measles vaccination is the effective population prevention.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The real patient has repeated brief myoclonic jerks with a strikingly periodic pattern. Core concept: Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis is a delayed progressive complication of measles infection. Pitfall: Periodic SSPE myoclonus should not be dismissed as random behavioral movement or isolated epilepsy.
anchors: EEG classically shows periodic high-voltage complexes time-locked to the myoclonus.
Raised measles antibody titers in CSF support the diagnosis.
differentiation: Suspect SSPE in a child or adolescent with progressive cognitive or behavioral decline and periodic myoclonus after prior measles. EEG shows periodic stereotyped high-voltage complexes, and raised measles antibody in CSF supports the diagnosis after other progressive encephalopathies are excluded.

title: Rigid bronchoscopy: foreign-body grasping and extraction animation
subject: Pediatrics / ENT
source: Rigid Bronchoscopy for Airway Foreign Body Removal | Emergency Respiratory Procedure in 3D | Channel: VOKA 3D Anatomy & Pathology
summary: The animation shows bronchoscopic forceps securing the airway foreign body and withdrawing it under direct vision.
identify: The exact window shows the bronchoscope approaching the object, grasping it, and withdrawing it under direct visualization.
examAngle: Rigid bronchoscopy provides airway control and is the standard therapeutic approach for many pediatric airway foreign bodies. NEET PG pitfall: A normal chest radiograph does not exclude a radiolucent aspirated foreign body.
management: Arrange urgent rigid bronchoscopy by an experienced airway team for significant suspected pediatric airway foreign body. Maintain ventilation, remove the object under vision, reinspect the airway, and treat edema, infection, or residual obstruction afterward.
quickRevision: At 01:10-01:25, watch for: The animation shows bronchoscopic forceps securing the airway foreign body and withdrawing it under direct vision. Core concept: Rigid bronchoscopy provides airway control and is the standard therapeutic approach for many pediatric airway foreign bodies. Pitfall: A normal chest radiograph does not exclude a radiolucent aspirated foreign body.
anchors: The object, forceps, and bronchoscope are withdrawn in a controlled unit when needed to prevent loss at the glottis.
After extraction, reinspect both bronchial trees for a second object, bleeding, edema, or residual obstruction.
differentiation: A sudden choking episode followed by unilateral wheeze, reduced air entry, or persistent cough strongly suggests aspiration even with a normal radiograph. Inspiratory and expiratory films or CT may show air trapping, but definitive diagnosis and treatment are bronchoscopic.

title: Pediatric skin-pinch test for dehydration
subject: Pediatrics
source: Signs of Dehydration- Loss of Skin Elasticity | Channel: MERIT INDIA
summary: On a real infant, abdominal skin is gently pinched and released while the examiner watches how quickly the fold returns.
identify: A real infant's abdominal skin is pinched and released repeatedly so the return of the skin fold can be judged directly, without instructional overlays.
examAngle: In WHO or IMCI assessment, a skin pinch returning very slowly, generally after more than two seconds, is one sign used to classify severe dehydration. NEET PG pitfall: The clip demonstrates the maneuver, not a complete dehydration classification; severe malnutrition, edema, and very young age can alter skin recoil.
management: Use oral rehydration for mild or moderate dehydration when the child can drink, continuing feeding and zinc for acute diarrhea as indicated. Severe dehydration or shock requires prompt protocol-based intravenous isotonic fluid with frequent reassessment and correction of glucose and electrolytes.
quickRevision: At 00:03-00:15, watch for: On a real infant, abdominal skin is gently pinched and released while the examiner watches how quickly the fold returns. Core concept: In WHO or IMCI assessment, a skin pinch returning very slowly, generally after more than two seconds, is one sign used to classify severe dehydration. Pitfall: The clip demonstrates the maneuver, not a complete dehydration classification; severe malnutrition, edema, and very young age can alter skin recoil.
anchors: Interpret it with mental state, sunken eyes, drinking or thirst, pulse, perfusion, urine output, and the overall clinical context.
Use the abdominal skin in a young child and observe the return rather than judging skin appearance in a single frame.
differentiation: Classify dehydration using mental state, eye appearance, thirst or drinking ability, skin-pinch return, perfusion, urine output, and weight change when known. Severe malnutrition and edema make the skin pinch less reliable, so the whole examination determines severity.

title: Positive Hoffmann sign
subject: Medicine / Neurology / Orthopedics
source: Hoffmann sign positive | Channel: Ortho Eval Pal with Paul Marquis PT
summary: Flicking the distal phalanx of the relaxed middle finger produces involuntary thumb flexion and adduction with index-finger flexion.
identify: Flicking the distal phalanx is followed by involuntary thumb and index-finger flexion; the stimulus-response sequence cannot be reduced to a still pose.
examAngle: A positive Hoffmann sign suggests corticospinal hyperexcitability in the upper limbs, often from cervical myelopathy. NEET PG pitfall: Finger withdrawal from discomfort is not a Hoffmann response; the characteristic thumb-index movement matters.
management: Progressive or function-limiting compressive myelopathy needs prompt spine or neurosurgical assessment and often decompression. Treat inflammatory, metabolic, or structural causes and use rehabilitation and fall precautions while avoiding risky neck manipulation.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: Flicking the distal phalanx of the relaxed middle finger produces involuntary thumb flexion and adduction with index-finger flexion. Core concept: A positive Hoffmann sign suggests corticospinal hyperexcitability in the upper limbs, often from cervical myelopathy. Pitfall: Finger withdrawal from discomfort is not a Hoffmann response; the characteristic thumb-index movement matters.
anchors: Interpret it with tone, reflexes, plantar responses, gait, hand dexterity, and sensory findings.
A bilateral response may occur in healthy hyperreflexic individuals and is not diagnostic by itself.
differentiation: A positive Hoffmann sign with hand clumsiness, gait imbalance, hyperreflexia, spasticity, or sensory change suggests cervical myelopathy. MRI of the cervical cord identifies compression, inflammation, tumor, or other pathology; isolated bilateral positivity can be physiologic.

title: Myerson glabellar-tap sign in Parkinson disease
subject: Medicine / Neurology
source: Myerson sign in early idiopathic Parkinson disease | Channel: Osama Shukir Muhammed Amin
summary: Repeated taps over the glabella cause persistent blinking without the normal habituation seen after the first few taps.
identify: Persistent blinking continues with repeated glabellar taps instead of habituating, so the diagnosis depends on a repeated temporal sequence.
examAngle: The Myerson sign is a frontal-release response commonly associated with Parkinson disease. NEET PG pitfall: Blinking to the first one or two taps is physiologic; persistence without habituation is the abnormal finding.
management: The glabellar response itself needs no treatment. Manage the underlying parkinsonism with cause-directed medication review, levodopa-based symptomatic therapy when appropriate, exercise, rehabilitation, and treatment of gait and nonmotor complications.
quickRevision: At 00:03-00:13, watch for: Repeated taps over the glabella cause persistent blinking without the normal habituation seen after the first few taps. Core concept: The Myerson sign is a frontal-release response commonly associated with Parkinson disease. Pitfall: Blinking to the first one or two taps is physiologic; persistence without habituation is the abnormal finding.
anchors: A normal subject usually stops blinking after several taps even though tapping continues.
The sign is neither sensitive nor specific enough to establish Parkinson disease without bradykinesia and the broader examination.
differentiation: Myerson sign is interpreted only as supportive evidence within a parkinsonian examination. Diagnose Parkinson disease from bradykinesia plus rigidity or rest tremor, and look for medication exposure, vascular disease, gaze palsy, autonomic failure, or other atypical features.

title: Posterior hip-dislocation reduction
subject: Orthopedics / Trauma
source: Reduction of posterior hip dislocation with push technique, Assoc. Prof. Sattaya Rojanasthien | Channel: Sattaya Rojanastien
summary: The anatomical animation demonstrates a closed reduction vector for posterior hip dislocation.
identify: Traction, counter-traction, hip flexion, controlled rotation, reduction, and post-reduction stability are a coordinated sequence.
examAngle: Posterior dislocation classically presents with a flexed, adducted, internally rotated limb. NEET PG pitfall: This is an anatomical animation, not footage of reduction in a real injured patient.
management: Perform urgent closed reduction under adequate sedation or anesthesia, ideally as early as possible to reduce osteonecrosis risk. Open reduction is required for irreducibility, instability, nonconcentric reduction, or associated fragments needing fixation; rehabilitation follows stability and injury pattern.
quickRevision: At 00:05-00:20, watch for: The anatomical animation demonstrates a closed reduction vector for posterior hip dislocation. Core concept: Posterior dislocation classically presents with a flexed, adducted, internally rotated limb. Pitfall: This is an anatomical animation, not footage of reduction in a real injured patient.
anchors: Record sciatic-nerve and vascular status before and after urgent reduction under adequate sedation or anesthesia.
Post-reduction radiographs and often CT assess congruity and associated fragments.
differentiation: Confirm posterior hip dislocation on anteroposterior pelvic imaging and examine the sciatic nerve and distal circulation before reduction. Look for femoral-head or acetabular fracture, and obtain post-reduction CT to assess congruity and intra-articular fragments.

title: Cerebellar rebound phenomenon
subject: Medicine / Neurology
source: Abnormal coordination exam: rebound | Channel: onlinemedicalvideo
summary: The patient resists elbow flexion; when resistance is suddenly released, the arm overshoots because the antagonist fails to check the movement.
identify: After resistance is suddenly released, the affected arm overshoots because antagonist braking is impaired; only the release and overshoot sequence is diagnostic.
examAngle: The rebound phenomenon reflects impaired timing of agonist-antagonist activity in cerebellar disease. NEET PG pitfall: An examiner who releases without guarding can create injury and cannot reliably distinguish true dysmetria from simple momentum.
management: Treat the cause, such as stroke, inflammation, tumor, toxicity, nutritional deficiency, or inherited disease. Physiotherapy, occupational therapy, gait aids, fall prevention, and speech or swallow care address persistent disability.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The patient resists elbow flexion; when resistance is suddenly released, the arm overshoots because the antagonist fails to check the movement. Core concept: The rebound phenomenon reflects impaired timing of agonist-antagonist activity in cerebellar disease. Pitfall: An examiner who releases without guarding can create injury and cannot reliably distinguish true dysmetria from simple momentum.
anchors: The abnormality is usually ipsilateral to a cerebellar-hemisphere lesion.
Protect the patient's face during testing and compare sides because weakness can also alter the response.
differentiation: Rebound is interpreted with dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, hypotonia, gait, eye movements, and speech. MRI of the posterior fossa and cause-directed laboratory or toxic evaluation are used when a new cerebellar syndrome is present.

title: Dynamic hip-screw insertion for an intertrochanteric fracture
subject: Orthopedics / Trauma
source: Femur Fracture Fixation (Dynamic Hip Screw Method) | Channel: Viz Medical
summary: The procedural animation shows drilling, lag-screw insertion, side-plate seating, and shaft-screw fixation of a dynamic hip screw.
identify: The selected animation shows drilling, lag-screw insertion, side-plate seating, and shaft-screw fixation without the introductory fracture images.
examAngle: A dynamic hip screw permits controlled collapse across many stable intertrochanteric fractures. NEET PG pitfall: Reverse-obliquity and subtrochanteric patterns are usually better treated with a cephalomedullary device.
management: Provide early fixation when medically possible, with analgesia, thromboprophylaxis, pressure-area care, delirium prevention, nutrition, and prompt mobilization. Confirm reduction, central lag-screw placement, and an acceptable tip-apex distance during surgery.
quickRevision: At 00:31-00:46, watch for: The procedural animation shows drilling, lag-screw insertion, side-plate seating, and shaft-screw fixation of a dynamic hip screw. Core concept: A dynamic hip screw permits controlled collapse across many stable intertrochanteric fractures. Pitfall: Reverse-obliquity and subtrochanteric patterns are usually better treated with a cephalomedullary device.
anchors: Place the lag screw centrally with an acceptable tip-apex distance, commonly below 25 mm.
Confirm reduction and implant position in orthogonal fluoroscopic views.
differentiation: Classify the intertrochanteric fracture on orthogonal radiographs and judge stability, lateral-wall integrity, subtrochanteric extension, and patient fitness. Dynamic hip screw fixation is most suitable for many stable patterns; unstable reverse-obliquity patterns usually need a cephalomedullary device.

title: Below-knee cast application: initial roll and molding
subject: Orthopedics
source: How to apply pop cast|| How to apply pop cast 5th metatarsal fracture|| | Channel: EARTH SONEMEHAR Medical
summary: The selected window shows the first circumferential cast rolls being applied and molded around the padded lower leg.
identify: The timestamp skips radiographs and begins with circumferential cast application and molding around the ankle and leg.
examAngle: Padding must protect bony prominences, and molding should support the fracture without creating focal pressure. NEET PG pitfall: Increasing pain, paresthesia, pallor, or pain on passive stretch after casting requires urgent reassessment.
management: Give elevation and cast-care instructions and arrange radiographic and clinical follow-up for displacement. Split or remove the cast urgently for escalating pain, neurologic or vascular change, pressure injury, or suspected compartment syndrome.
quickRevision: At 01:05-01:20, watch for: The selected window shows the first circumferential cast rolls being applied and molded around the padded lower leg. Core concept: Padding must protect bony prominences, and molding should support the fracture without creating focal pressure. Pitfall: Increasing pain, paresthesia, pallor, or pain on passive stretch after casting requires urgent reassessment.
anchors: Keep the ankle in the intended position and smooth or evert the cast edges before the material hardens.
Document distal pulses, capillary refill, movement, and sensation before and after immobilization.
differentiation: Document fracture alignment and a complete distal neurovascular examination before and after the cast. Check that padding, joint position, molding, and cast length match the injury and that swelling has room without loss of support.

title: Asymmetric tonic-neck reflex in an infant
subject: Pediatrics / Neonatology
source: Newborn tonic neck reflex | Channel: LA Occupational Therapy
summary: Turning the infant's head to one side produces extension of the face-side arm and leg with flexion on the occiput side.
identify: Turning the infant's head produces extension of the face-side limbs and flexion of the skull-side limbs, a posture that emerges through movement.
examAngle: The asymmetric tonic-neck reflex is the primitive fencing posture generated by head rotation. NEET PG pitfall: Voluntary looking with incidental limb movement is not ATNR; the patterned posture should follow passive head rotation.
management: No treatment is needed for a normal transient reflex. Abnormal persistence or asymmetry requires developmental and neurologic assessment, hearing and vision review when indicated, and early goal-directed physiotherapy or occupational therapy.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: Turning the infant's head to one side produces extension of the face-side arm and leg with flexion on the occiput side. Core concept: The asymmetric tonic-neck reflex is the primitive fencing posture generated by head rotation. Pitfall: Voluntary looking with incidental limb movement is not ATNR; the patterned posture should follow passive head rotation.
anchors: It appears in early infancy and normally integrates by roughly four to six months as cortical control develops.
Persistence can interfere with rolling, hand-to-midline activity, and later motor development and is seen in cerebral palsy.
differentiation: Elicit ATNR gently and assess whether head rotation reliably produces the fencing posture. Persistence beyond the expected age, obligatory posturing, asymmetry, delayed hand-to-midline activity, or abnormal tone supports a broader motor-development disorder rather than an isolated reflex finding.

title: Positive Nikolsky sign in a real patient
subject: Dermatology
source: How to demonstrate Nikolsky's sign? | Channel: Dermacoaching by Dr. Niharika Jha
summary: In a real patient close-up, gentle tangential pressure on clinically affected skin produces visible superficial epidermal separation.
identify: The close-up shows tangential pressure on involved skin followed by visible superficial epidermal separation in a real patient, with no teaching overlay.
examAngle: A positive Nikolsky sign reflects loss of cohesion within the superficial epidermis or at the dermoepidermal junction. NEET PG pitfall: Nikolsky positivity is not disease-specific, and the sign should be elicited with gentle tangential pressure rather than forceful rubbing.
management: Treat pemphigus vulgaris with systemic corticosteroid-sparing therapy, commonly rituximab with corticosteroids, plus wound, infection, oral, nutritional, and thrombosis care. Extensive epidermal necrolysis or burns require emergency supportive management rather than pemphigus treatment.
quickRevision: At 01:03-01:18, watch for: In a real patient close-up, gentle tangential pressure on clinically affected skin produces visible superficial epidermal separation. Core concept: A positive Nikolsky sign reflects loss of cohesion within the superficial epidermis or at the dermoepidermal junction. Pitfall: Nikolsky positivity is not disease-specific, and the sign should be elicited with gentle tangential pressure rather than forceful rubbing.
anchors: It may occur in pemphigus, staphylococcal scalded-skin syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, and other severe superficial blistering disorders.
The underlying disease is confirmed from the full clinical pattern and appropriate lesional histology, perilesional direct immunofluorescence, microbiology, or drug assessment.
differentiation: Nikolsky sign is elicited on clinically suitable skin with gentle tangential pressure, then confirmed diagnostically by lesional histology and perilesional direct immunofluorescence. Pemphigus vulgaris shows suprabasal acantholysis and intercellular IgG or C3; burns and toxic epidermal necrolysis are important alternatives.

title: Leprosy slit-skin smear sampling
subject: Dermatology
source: Leprosy Physical Exam - Skin Smear | Channel: GLODERM
summary: The clinician demonstrates slit-skin smear sampling from a selected site in a patient being assessed for leprosy.
identify: Site selection, blanching, incision, scraping, slide transfer, and hemostasis must be performed in order.
examAngle: Common sites include active lesion edges and cool areas such as earlobes. NEET PG pitfall: Use infection-control and sharps precautions and document the exact sampling sites.
management: Use WHO multidrug therapy according to paucibacillary or multibacillary classification, with rifampicin, dapsone, and clofazimine as indicated. Treat type 1 neuritis promptly with corticosteroids and manage erythema nodosum leprosum with specialist anti-inflammatory therapy while continuing multidrug treatment.
quickRevision: At 00:33-00:42, watch for: The clinician demonstrates slit-skin smear sampling from a selected site in a patient being assessed for leprosy. Core concept: Common sites include active lesion edges and cool areas such as earlobes. Pitfall: Use infection-control and sharps precautions and document the exact sampling sites.
anchors: A small slit is made, tissue fluid is scraped without drawing free blood, and the material is spread on a slide.
Modified acid-fast staining supports the bacteriological and morphological indices.
differentiation: Sample active sites correctly, stain for acid-fast bacilli, and report bacteriological and morphologic indices when used. Slit-skin smear is most useful in multibacillary disease; diagnosis also depends on sensory loss, nerve thickening or deficit, lesions, and biopsy when needed.

title: Bulla-spread sign in bullous pemphigoid
subject: Dermatology
source: Bulla spread sign in a patient with bullous pemphigoid | Channel: Dermacoaching by Dr. Niharika Jha
summary: Gentle downward pressure on the roof of an intact tense bulla makes fluid spread laterally beyond its original margin.
identify: Gentle pressure on the roof of an intact bulla visibly extends it into adjacent skin; the direction of spread under pressure is the sign.
examAngle: The bulla-spread or Asboe-Hansen sign indicates mechanical extension of a blister within its cleavage plane. NEET PG pitfall: Do not rupture the blister aggressively or treat this sign as disease-specific.
management: Bullous pemphigoid is treated with potent topical or systemic corticosteroid-based regimens and selected steroid-sparing therapy; doxycycline is useful in some patients. Provide wound and infection care and tailor treatment after the specific immunobullous diagnosis is confirmed.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: Gentle downward pressure on the roof of an intact tense bulla makes fluid spread laterally beyond its original margin. Core concept: The bulla-spread or Asboe-Hansen sign indicates mechanical extension of a blister within its cleavage plane. Pitfall: Do not rupture the blister aggressively or treat this sign as disease-specific.
anchors: It may occur in more than one autoimmune blistering disorder and does not by itself distinguish bullous pemphigoid from pemphigus.
Diagnosis requires biopsy of a fresh lesion for histology and perilesional skin for direct immunofluorescence.
differentiation: Record blister tension, mucosal involvement, Nikolsky response, and bulla spread, but do not diagnose from bedside signs alone. Histology of a fresh blister and direct immunofluorescence of perilesional skin distinguish subepidermal bullous pemphigoid from intraepidermal pemphigus and other blistering disorders.

title: Strongyloides stercoralis: larval motility in stool
subject: Microbiology / Parasitology
source: Strongyloides larvae in stool | Channel: Atul Aher
summary: A slender Strongyloides larva actively bends and advances through the fresh stool microscopic field.
identify: The larva actively flexes and changes position in the fresh stool preparation; live movement is the essential video-only finding.
examAngle: Rhabditiform larvae, rather than eggs, are usually found in fresh stool in uncomplicated strongyloidiasis. NEET PG pitfall: Differentiate Strongyloides larvae from hookworm by buccal cavity and genital-primordium morphology, not by motion alone.
management: Ivermectin is first-line treatment for uncomplicated strongyloidiasis. Hyperinfection or dissemination requires urgent daily therapy with repeated parasitologic clearance, reduction of immunosuppression when possible, and treatment of gram-negative sepsis and organ complications.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: A slender Strongyloides larva actively bends and advances through the fresh stool microscopic field. Core concept: Rhabditiform larvae, rather than eggs, are usually found in fresh stool in uncomplicated strongyloidiasis. Pitfall: Differentiate Strongyloides larvae from hookworm by buccal cavity and genital-primordium morphology, not by motion alone.
anchors: Repeated stool examination, concentration, agar-plate culture, or molecular testing improves sensitivity.
Autoinfection permits lifelong infection and can cause fatal hyperinfection after corticosteroids or other immunosuppression.
differentiation: Search repeated stool specimens for rhabditiform larvae using concentration, agar-plate culture, or molecular testing, and use serology when appropriate. Before corticosteroids or transplantation, screen exposed patients because asymptomatic infection can become disseminated.

title: Hair-pull test for telogen effluvium
subject: Dermatology
source: Hair pull test technique |Telogen effluvium |Alopecia | Channel: Dermatology mnemonics + AMC
summary: The examiner gently grips a bundle of scalp hair and pulls to assess active shedding.
identify: The number of hairs released after a standardized gentle traction is the sign; a photograph of alopecia cannot substitute.
examAngle: Sample roughly 40 to 60 hairs from several scalp areas and apply steady traction from proximal to distal. NEET PG pitfall: Recent shampooing and the number of hairs grasped affect the result, so standardize the technique.
management: Correct the trigger or deficiency and reassure that acute telogen effluvium often regrows over months. Persistent or patterned loss needs dermatologic reassessment; topical minoxidil may be considered in chronic shedding or coexisting pattern alopecia.
quickRevision: At 00:14-00:20, watch for: The examiner gently grips a bundle of scalp hair and pulls to assess active shedding. Core concept: Sample roughly 40 to 60 hairs from several scalp areas and apply steady traction from proximal to distal. Pitfall: Recent shampooing and the number of hairs grasped affect the result, so standardize the technique.
anchors: Extraction of more than about 10 percent of grasped hairs is commonly considered positive.
Telogen effluvium yields club hairs and usually produces diffuse shedding several weeks after a trigger.
differentiation: A positive hair-pull test supports active shedding but does not establish the cause. Determine timing after fever, childbirth, surgery, weight loss, medication, or stress, examine the scalp, and selectively test blood count, ferritin, thyroid function, or other suspected deficiencies.

title: Tzanck smear: scraping the base of a fresh vesicle
subject: Dermatology / Pathology
source: Tzanck smear | Channel: Dermacoaching by Dr. Niharika Jha
summary: The roof of a fresh vesicle is opened and the moist base is gently scraped before material is transferred to a slide.
identify: The selected 10 seconds show only active scraping of the moist lesion base, before the video returns to text slides.
examAngle: A fresh intact vesicle provides the highest cellular yield; crusted or treated lesions are often nondiagnostic. NEET PG pitfall: Sampling crust or blister fluid instead of scraping the lesion base commonly produces a false-negative smear.
management: Start an appropriate antiviral promptly for significant HSV or zoster, with intravenous acyclovir for severe, disseminated, neonatal, encephalitic, or sight-threatening disease. Provide analgesia, eye referral for ocular involvement, and infection-control counseling.
quickRevision: At 00:30-00:40, watch for: The roof of a fresh vesicle is opened and the moist base is gently scraped before material is transferred to a slide. Core concept: A fresh intact vesicle provides the highest cellular yield; crusted or treated lesions are often nondiagnostic. Pitfall: Sampling crust or blister fluid instead of scraping the lesion base commonly produces a false-negative smear.
anchors: Multinucleated giant cells with molding and chromatin margination support a herpetic infection.
Tzanck cytology cannot reliably distinguish herpes simplex virus from varicella-zoster virus.
differentiation: Tzanck smear can rapidly show herpetic cytopathic change but cannot identify HSV type or distinguish HSV from varicella-zoster virus. Confirm uncertain, severe, neonatal, ocular, or immunocompromised infection with lesion PCR or another specific test.

title: KOH skin scraping for dermatophytosis
subject: Dermatology / Microbiology
source: Skin Scraping for Fungal identification KOH | Channel: dr. Adianto Jaya Nagara, Sp. DVE
summary: The direct procedure selects an active scaly edge, scrapes material onto a slide, adds KOH, warms gently, and examines the preparation.
identify: The active edge must be selected, scraped onto a slide, treated with potassium hydroxide, and examined systematically for fungal elements.
examAngle: Scrape the advancing scaly margin rather than the inactive center of a tinea lesion. NEET PG pitfall: Do not identify air bubbles or textile fibers as fungal hyphae; trace true refractile branching structures through the field.
management: Use a topical antifungal for limited skin disease and an oral agent for extensive, hair, nail, or refractory infection after checking interactions and contraindications. Keep the area dry, treat relevant contacts or reservoirs, and avoid irrational topical steroid-antifungal combinations.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The direct procedure selects an active scaly edge, scrapes material onto a slide, adds KOH, warms gently, and examines the preparation. Core concept: Scrape the advancing scaly margin rather than the inactive center of a tinea lesion. Pitfall: Do not identify air bubbles or textile fibers as fungal hyphae; trace true refractile branching structures through the field.
anchors: Potassium hydroxide clears keratin so branching septate hyphae can be seen.
Insufficient sample, prior topical treatment, and poor focusing can produce a false-negative mount.
differentiation: Demonstrate branching septate hyphae on KOH from the active scaly edge and use culture or molecular identification when the diagnosis is uncertain, extensive, recurrent, or treatment-resistant. Exclude candidiasis, erythrasma, eczema, psoriasis, and steroid-modified tinea.

title: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis: failed gastric passage on real-time ultrasound
subject: Radiodiagnosis / Pediatrics
source: ULTRASOUND - CONGENITAL HYPERTROPHIC PYLORIC STENOSIS 2 | Channel: BARODA MEDICAL COLLEGE - RADIOLOGY
summary: The ultrasound demonstrates an elongated thickened pyloric canal with little or no gastric passage.
identify: Persistent closure and failure of gastric contents to traverse the pylorus must be watched in real time.
examAngle: Typical cutoffs are pyloric muscle thickness above about 3 mm and channel length above about 15 to 18 mm. NEET PG pitfall: Do not rush to surgery before correcting the metabolic alkalosis.
management: Pyloric stenosis is a medical stabilization problem before it is a surgical one: stop feeds, decompress when needed, and correct dehydration, chloride, potassium, and alkalosis. Definitive treatment is pyloromyotomy after biochemical resuscitation.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:11, watch for: The ultrasound demonstrates an elongated thickened pyloric canal with little or no gastric passage. Core concept: Typical cutoffs are pyloric muscle thickness above about 3 mm and channel length above about 15 to 18 mm. Pitfall: Do not rush to surgery before correcting the metabolic alkalosis.
anchors: Infants present with progressive nonbilious projectile vomiting and may develop hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis.
Correct dehydration and electrolyte disturbance before pyloromyotomy.
differentiation: Ultrasound shows an elongated thickened pylorus with failure of gastric contents to pass during observation. Interpret measurements with age and local criteria, and confirm the typical nonbilious projectile vomiting, visible peristalsis, weight change, and hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis.

title: Aortic dissection: mobile intimal flap on echocardiography
subject: Radiodiagnosis / Cardiology
source: Aortic Aneurysm and Dissection | Channel: NEJM Group
summary: The echocardiographic clip shows a mobile intimal flap separating true and false aortic lumens.
identify: Independent flap movement and two-lumen flow across cardiac cycles make cine imaging essential.
examAngle: Stanford type A involves the ascending aorta and usually requires emergency surgery. NEET PG pitfall: A negative transthoracic study does not exclude dissection; use definitive aortic imaging when suspicion remains high.
management: Begin immediate analgesia and anti-impulse therapy with heart-rate control followed by blood-pressure control. Stanford type A dissection requires emergency surgery; uncomplicated type B disease is managed medically with surveillance, while rupture, malperfusion, expansion, or refractory symptoms prompt endovascular or surgical intervention.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The echocardiographic clip shows a mobile intimal flap separating true and false aortic lumens. Core concept: Stanford type A involves the ascending aorta and usually requires emergency surgery. Pitfall: A negative transthoracic study does not exclude dissection; use definitive aortic imaging when suspicion remains high.
anchors: TEE is valuable in unstable patients and can assess aortic regurgitation, pericardial effusion, and proximal extension.
Initial medical treatment reduces shear with rapid beta-blockade followed by blood-pressure control.
differentiation: A mobile intimal flap creates true and false lumens, but the entire aorta must be classified by CT angiography, transesophageal echo, or MRI according to stability. Assess pulse or pressure asymmetry, aortic regurgitation, pericardial effusion, coronary compromise, branch ischemia, and rupture.

title: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy: apical ballooning on echocardiography
subject: Medicine / Cardiology
source: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy | Channel: Mastering and Guidelines in Ultrasound and Echo-Card
summary: The echocardiographic loop shows marked apical and mid-ventricular hypokinesia with a ballooned apex and relatively vigorous basal contraction.
identify: Basal contraction is preserved while the mid-apical segments remain akinetic or dyskinetic across systole, creating dynamic apical ballooning.
examAngle: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy is a transient stress-associated left-ventricular systolic dysfunction pattern that often extends beyond one coronary territory. NEET PG pitfall: Do not diagnose Takotsubo from apical ballooning alone; myocarditis and coronary ischemia must be excluded.
management: Treat heart failure, shock, and arrhythmia according to the hemodynamic pattern while avoiding harmful catecholamine escalation when dynamic outflow obstruction is present. Anticoagulate selected patients with apical thrombus or high thrombotic risk and arrange follow-up imaging to document recovery.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: The echocardiographic loop shows marked apical and mid-ventricular hypokinesia with a ballooned apex and relatively vigorous basal contraction. Core concept: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy is a transient stress-associated left-ventricular systolic dysfunction pattern that often extends beyond one coronary territory. Pitfall: Do not diagnose Takotsubo from apical ballooning alone; myocarditis and coronary ischemia must be excluded.
anchors: It commonly affects postmenopausal women after emotional or physical stress and can mimic acute coronary syndrome.
Coronary evaluation and clinical criteria are needed because the echo pattern alone cannot safely exclude an acute coronary occlusion.
differentiation: Takotsubo is diagnosed after evaluating the acute-coronary-syndrome presentation with ECG, troponin, echocardiography, and coronary imaging, and after considering myocarditis. Repeat imaging should show recovery of ventricular function; assess for LV outflow obstruction, apical thrombus, and arrhythmia.

title: Achalasia: delayed emptying on cine barium swallow
subject: Radiodiagnosis / Gastroenterology
source: A barium swallow showing a birds beak - Achalasia | Channel: Achalasia Support UK
summary: The cine barium swallow shows retained contrast tapering toward distal bird-beak narrowing with delayed esophageal emptying.
identify: The diagnostic motion is retained contrast descending slowly toward fixed distal bird-beak narrowing during a swallow sequence.
examAngle: Achalasia combines impaired lower-esophageal-sphincter relaxation with absent normal peristalsis. NEET PG pitfall: A bird-beak barium appearance supports achalasia but does not replace manometric confirmation.
management: Durable treatment options include pneumatic dilation, laparoscopic Heller myotomy with partial fundoplication, and peroral endoscopic myotomy, selected by subtype and expertise. Botulinum toxin is generally reserved for patients who cannot undergo definitive therapy.
quickRevision: At 00:08-00:23, watch for: The cine barium swallow shows retained contrast tapering toward distal bird-beak narrowing with delayed esophageal emptying. Core concept: Achalasia combines impaired lower-esophageal-sphincter relaxation with absent normal peristalsis. Pitfall: A bird-beak barium appearance supports achalasia but does not replace manometric confirmation.
anchors: High-resolution manometry is the confirmatory test and defines achalasia subtype.
Endoscopy is needed to exclude pseudoachalasia or a mechanical obstructing lesion.
differentiation: Barium swallow may show delayed emptying and bird-beak narrowing, but high-resolution manometry confirms impaired EGJ relaxation and absent peristalsis and defines subtype. Endoscopy is required to exclude a mechanical lesion or pseudoachalasia, especially with rapid weight loss or older age.

title: Hysterosalpingography tubal spill: 3D procedural animation
subject: Radiodiagnosis / OBGYN

summary: The labeled 3D sequence shows contrast filling the uterine cavity, traversing both tubes, and spilling freely into the peritoneum.
identify: The animation must show contrast progressing from the uterine cavity through both tubes and dispersing freely into the peritoneum.
examAngle: Free bilateral peritoneal spill indicates tubal patency at the time of the study. NEET PG pitfall: Contrast intravasation into uterine veins is not the same as free intraperitoneal tubal spill.
management: No treatment is required for normal free spill. Apparent proximal block may need repeat selective cannulation, while persistent tubal disease is integrated with age, ovarian reserve, semen analysis, laparoscopy findings, and options such as tubal surgery or IVF.
quickRevision: At 01:05-01:15, watch for: The labeled 3D sequence shows contrast filling the uterine cavity, traversing both tubes, and spilling freely into the peritoneum. anchors: Transient cornual spasm can simulate proximal obstruction.

differentiation: Interpret bilateral spill only after confirming a well-outlined uterine cavity and tubal filling; transient cornual spasm can mimic proximal occlusion and intravasation can mimic spill. HSG is performed in the follicular phase after pregnancy and active pelvic infection are excluded.

title: Needle cricothyrotomy on a simulator
subject: Anaesthesiology / Emergency Medicine

management: Connect to a pressure-controlled oxygen source with adequate expiratory time while the team establishes a definitive airway. This technique ventilates poorly when expiration is obstructed and must be converted promptly to an appropriate tracheal airway.
quickRevision: At 00:30-00:42, watch for: The simulator demonstrates needle entry through the cricothyroid membrane and cannula placement. anchors: Advance a large-bore cannula caudally while aspirating for air, then secure it and connect an oxygenation system.
Needle cricothyrotomy is a temporary rescue in a cannot-intubate, cannot-oxygenate emergency.
differentiation: Needle cricothyrotomy is a temporary rescue oxygenation technique for a cannot-intubate, cannot-oxygenate emergency, especially in a small child when a surgical airway is unsuitable. Confirm landmarks and continuous gas egress, and watch for subcutaneous emphysema, posterior-wall injury, and barotrauma.

title: Nasopharyngeal-airway insertion
subject: Anaesthesiology
source: Nasopharyngeal Airway Insertion | Doczero E-learning | Channel: doczeroElearning
summary: The demonstration shows lubrication, nasal insertion along the floor, and rotation of a nasopharyngeal airway.

examAngle: Estimate size from the nostril to the tragus or angle of the mandible and choose a diameter that fits without force. NEET PG pitfall: Forcing an oversized airway can cause epistaxis and turbinate injury.
management: Lubricate and advance along the nasal floor with the bevel oriented safely, stopping for resistance or significant bleeding. The device only bypasses upper-airway soft tissue; continue oxygenation, ventilation, suction, monitoring, and escalation to a definitive airway as needed.
quickRevision: At 00:08-00:18, watch for: The demonstration shows lubrication, nasal insertion along the floor, and rotation of a nasopharyngeal airway. Core concept: Estimate size from the nostril to the tragus or angle of the mandible and choose a diameter that fits without force. Pitfall: Forcing an oversized airway can cause epistaxis and turbinate injury.
anchors: Insert along the nasal floor with the bevel toward the septum, using gentle rotation if needed.
An NPA can be tolerated with an intact gag reflex but requires caution in major mid-face or suspected skull-base trauma.
differentiation: Size the nasopharyngeal airway from nostril to tragus or angle of jaw and choose a diameter that passes without force. Avoid nasal insertion with major midface trauma, suspected skull-base injury, severe coagulopathy, or obstructed nasal anatomy.

title: Recovery position: rolling an unresponsive breathing adult
subject: Anaesthesiology / Emergency Medicine
source: How to put someone into the recovery position | NHS | Channel: NHS
summary: The rescuer positions the limbs and rolls an unresponsive but breathing adult onto the side while keeping the airway open.
identify: The timestamp starts after rescue-breathing footage and shows the continuous roll into the lateral recovery position.
examAngle: Use the recovery position when normal breathing is present and no condition requires immediate supine treatment. NEET PG pitfall: Do not place a patient with absent or agonal breathing in the recovery position instead of starting CPR.
management: Call emergency services, keep the airway dependent, prevent heat loss, and monitor breathing continuously. If breathing becomes absent or abnormal, return the patient supine and start CPR; roll back promptly if vomiting, trauma, or deterioration requires intervention.
quickRevision: At 00:35-00:50, watch for: The rescuer positions the limbs and rolls an unresponsive but breathing adult onto the side while keeping the airway open. Core concept: Use the recovery position when normal breathing is present and no condition requires immediate supine treatment. Pitfall: Do not place a patient with absent or agonal breathing in the recovery position instead of starting CPR.
anchors: Support the cheek, flex the far knee, roll the patient as one controlled movement, and tilt the head to maintain airway drainage.
Continue to monitor breathing and be ready to return the patient supine and begin CPR if breathing becomes abnormal.
differentiation: Use the recovery position only after confirming unresponsiveness with normal breathing and no immediate need for spinal immobilization or supine treatment. Recheck airway, breathing, circulation, injury, glucose, and likely cause after positioning.

title: Double-burst ulnar-nerve stimulation: visible adductor-pollicis response
subject: Anaesthesiology
source: Double burst stimuli | Channel: Jeffrey Moore
summary: Two closely spaced ulnar-nerve stimulus bursts produce visible thumb adduction responses whose relative fade can be compared.

examAngle: Double-burst stimulation makes tactile or visible fade easier to detect than a simple train-of-four in moderate residual nondepolarizing block. NEET PG pitfall: Do not use visible double-burst response alone to certify complete neuromuscular recovery.
management: Allow spontaneous recovery or give an appropriate reversal agent, such as sugammadex for compatible aminosteroid block or neostigmine at a suitable depth with antimuscarinic protection. Continue ventilation and airway protection until objective recovery is confirmed.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:10, watch for: Two closely spaced ulnar-nerve stimulus bursts produce visible thumb adduction responses whose relative fade can be compared. anchors: The adductor pollicis response reflects ulnar-nerve stimulation, but qualitative absence of fade does not prove a train-of-four ratio of at least 0.9.
Quantitative neuromuscular monitoring is preferred before extubation when residual block is a concern.
differentiation: Qualitative double-burst fade indicates residual nondepolarizing neuromuscular block, but no visible fade cannot exclude clinically important weakness. Quantitative acceleromyography or electromyography at the adductor pollicis should document a train-of-four ratio of at least 0.9 before extubation.

title: Waveform capnography after tracheal intubation
subject: Anaesthesiology
source: Waveform capnography demonstration | Channel: ACLSTrainingCenter
summary: A sustained exhaled-carbon-dioxide waveform confirms ventilation through a tracheal tube.
identify: Persistent exhaled-CO2 waveforms over successive breaths confirm tracheal placement; one number or frame cannot.
examAngle: Continuous waveform capnography is the most reliable bedside confirmation of tracheal placement. NEET PG pitfall: Very low pulmonary blood flow can reduce exhaled CO2, but a persistently absent waveform still demands immediate tube reassessment.
management: Remove and reoxygenate after unrecognized esophageal intubation. During CPR, use capnography to monitor ventilation, compression effectiveness, and a sudden rise suggesting return of spontaneous circulation, while correcting equipment and physiologic causes of an abnormal trace.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: A sustained exhaled-carbon-dioxide waveform confirms ventilation through a tracheal tube. Pitfall: Very low pulmonary blood flow can reduce exhaled CO2, but a persistently absent waveform still demands immediate tube reassessment.
anchors: Esophageal placement may produce only a few transient low waves that rapidly disappear.
During CPR, ETCO2 also reflects perfusion and can rise abruptly with return of spontaneous circulation.
differentiation: A persistent exhaled carbon-dioxide waveform over several breaths is the best immediate confirmation that a tracheal tube is in the airway when pulmonary blood flow is present. Sudden waveform loss suggests displacement, disconnection, obstruction, apnea, or profound low flow and demands immediate assessment.

title: Lumbar-puncture needle insertion on a simulator
subject: Anaesthesiology / Medicine
source: Lumbar Puncture Simulator | Channel: Anatomy Warehouse
summary: The close-up shows a lumbar-puncture needle entering and advancing through the back pad of a procedural simulator.

examAngle: Adult lumbar puncture is commonly performed at L3-L4 or L4-L5, below the usual termination of the spinal cord. NEET PG pitfall: The clip is a simulator insertion close-up; it does not demonstrate patient positioning, sterile preparation, CSF return, opening-pressure measurement, or a complete real-patient procedure.
management: Do not delay empiric antibiotics for suspected bacterial meningitis when LP or imaging cannot be performed promptly; obtain blood cultures and treat first. Manage post-dural-puncture headache conservatively initially, with epidural blood patch for severe persistent cases when appropriate.
quickRevision: At 00:34-00:46, watch for: The close-up shows a lumbar-puncture needle entering and advancing through the back pad of a procedural simulator. anchors: The midline path crosses skin, subcutaneous tissue, supraspinous and interspinous ligaments, ligamentum flavum, epidural space, dura, and arachnoid.
Opening pressure is measured accurately in the lateral decubitus position with the patient relaxed and legs not tightly flexed.
differentiation: Before lumbar puncture, confirm the indication and assess for focal neurologic deficit, papilledema, severe coagulopathy, local infection, cardiorespiratory instability, or a mass-lesion risk requiring imaging first. title: Patellar reflex assessment during magnesium sulfate therapy
subject: Obstetrics / Clinical Examination
source: Patellar Reflex Test | Knee Reflex Examination of Deep Tendons | Channel: RegisteredNurseRN
summary: The bedside clip shows a relaxed leg, patellar-tendon strike, and the immediate quadriceps contraction and knee extension.
identify: The tendon strike and immediate knee extension form a brief visible test; loss of the reflex is an early toxicity warning.
examAngle: The patellar reflex tests mainly L2-L4 through the femoral nerve, especially L4. NEET PG pitfall: An absent knee jerk can result from poor relaxation, faulty technique, neuropathy, or radiculopathy; interpret it in context.
management: Stop magnesium immediately when toxicity is suspected, support ventilation, and give intravenous calcium gluconate as the antidote according to emergency protocol. Reassess dose and interval in renal impairment and continue obstetric seizure and blood-pressure management safely.
quickRevision: At 00:36-00:47, watch for: The bedside clip shows a relaxed leg, patellar-tendon strike, and the immediate quadriceps contraction and knee extension. Core concept: The patellar reflex tests mainly L2-L4 through the femoral nerve, especially L4. Pitfall: An absent knee jerk can result from poor relaxation, faulty technique, neuropathy, or radiculopathy; interpret it in context.
anchors: During magnesium sulfate therapy, loss of deep-tendon reflexes can precede respiratory depression.
Suspected magnesium toxicity requires stopping magnesium, checking respiration and renal function, and giving intravenous calcium gluconate when indicated.
differentiation: During magnesium sulfate therapy, monitor patellar reflexes, respiratory rate, urine output, consciousness, and renal function. Loss of reflexes suggests toxicity but is not the only criterion; respiratory depression and conduction or cardiac effects occur at greater exposure.

title: Positive head-impulse test in vestibular neuritis
subject: ENT / Neurology
source: Head Impulse Test and Left Positive Saccade by Vestibular Neuritis | Channel: ENT Doctor Lorin Philippe
summary: A rapid head turn toward the affected side produces a corrective refixation saccade.
identify: A corrective saccade appears only after a rapid, unpredictable head turn toward the affected side.
examAngle: An abnormal head-impulse test indicates impaired vestibulo-ocular reflex on the side toward which the head is turned. NEET PG pitfall: HINTS is intended for continuous acute vestibular syndrome in trained hands, not brief positional dizziness.
management: Treat vestibular neuritis with early mobilization and vestibular rehabilitation; use vestibular suppressants and antiemetics only briefly. Selected early cases receive corticosteroids, while suspected posterior-circulation stroke follows an emergency stroke pathway.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: A rapid head turn toward the affected side produces a corrective refixation saccade. Core concept: An abnormal head-impulse test indicates impaired vestibulo-ocular reflex on the side toward which the head is turned. Pitfall: HINTS is intended for continuous acute vestibular syndrome in trained hands, not brief positional dizziness.
anchors: In acute vestibular syndrome, an abnormal unilateral test supports peripheral vestibular neuritis.
The maneuver uses small unpredictable rotations while the patient fixes on a target.
differentiation: A corrective catch-up saccade after a rapid head turn indicates deficient vestibulo-ocular reflex on that side and supports peripheral vestibular hypofunction. In acute vestibular syndrome, HINTS is reliable only in trained hands; direction-changing nystagmus, skew, severe ataxia, or central risk requires urgent stroke imaging.

title: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia
subject: Medicine / Neurology / Ophthalmology
source: Test of extraocular muscle function in a patient with internuclear ophthalmoplegia | Channel: NEJM Group
summary: On horizontal gaze, one eye fails to adduct while the abducting fellow eye develops nystagmus; convergence can remain intact.

examAngle: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia results from a lesion of the medial longitudinal fasciculus on the side of the adduction deficit. NEET PG pitfall: Name the lesion from the eye that cannot adduct, not from the eye with abducting nystagmus.
management: Treat the cause, including acute-stroke care or multiple-sclerosis relapse therapy when indicated. Diplopia may improve with recovery; temporary occlusion, prisms, botulinum toxin, or strabismus surgery are selected for persistent symptomatic misalignment.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: On horizontal gaze, one eye fails to adduct while the abducting fellow eye develops nystagmus; convergence can remain intact. Core concept: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia results from a lesion of the medial longitudinal fasciculus on the side of the adduction deficit. Pitfall: Name the lesion from the eye that cannot adduct, not from the eye with abducting nystagmus.
anchors: Multiple sclerosis commonly causes bilateral INO in younger adults, while brainstem ischemia often causes unilateral INO in older adults.
Preserved convergence helps distinguish a classic INO from some third-nerve or medial-rectus disorders, although it is not invariably present.
differentiation: Localize the lesion to the medial longitudinal fasciculus on the side of failed adduction and examine convergence, vertical gaze, pupils, ptosis, facial sensation, and other brainstem signs. MRI evaluates demyelination, infarction, tumor, and inflammation; vascular evaluation depends on onset and risk.

title: Functional endoscopic sinus surgery
subject: ENT

summary: The timestamp opens directly on intranasal endoscopic anatomy and instrument use during functional endoscopic sinus surgery.

examAngle: FESS restores ventilation and drainage by opening diseased sinus pathways while preserving mucosa where possible. NEET PG pitfall: The corrected timestamp bypasses faculty credits and lands on the operative endoscopic view.
management: Operate with mucosal preservation and restoration of sinus ventilation and drainage, then provide saline irrigation, topical intranasal corticosteroid therapy, and endoscopic follow-up or debridement. Bleeding, CSF leak, orbital injury, and recurrent inflammation require prompt recognition.
quickRevision: At 00:45-01:00, watch for: The timestamp opens directly on intranasal endoscopic anatomy and instrument use during functional endoscopic sinus surgery. anchors: Key danger boundaries include the lamina papyracea, skull base, orbit, optic nerve, and internal carotid artery.
Preoperative CT is the surgical road map for anatomic variants and disease extent.
differentiation: Functional endoscopic sinus surgery is planned from nasal endoscopy and thin-cut CT after the diagnosis and extent of chronic rhinosinusitis or a focal surgical lesion are established. Review orbit and skull-base anatomy and optimize inflammation before operating.

title: Type-I tympanoplasty: transcanal graft placement animation
subject: ENT
source: Eardrum Hole Repair Animation | Channel: Fauquier ENT

examAngle: Type-I tympanoplasty repairs the tympanic membrane when the ossicular chain is intact. NEET PG pitfall: The clip shows graft placement only, not proof of postoperative graft uptake or hearing improvement.
management: Repair with a suitable underlay or overlay graft technique and maintain stable support and ear-canal care. Keep the ear dry, treat infection, avoid pressure trauma, and reassess graft uptake and hearing; persistent perforation or cholesteatoma needs further specialist management.
quickRevision: At 00:27-00:42, watch for: The animation shows a tympanic-membrane graft being guided through the ear canal and positioned beneath the perforation. Core concept: Type-I tympanoplasty repairs the tympanic membrane when the ossicular chain is intact. Pitfall: The clip shows graft placement only, not proof of postoperative graft uptake or hearing improvement.
anchors: The underlay graft is supported against the remnant membrane while avoiding medialization, lateralization, or anterior blunting.
A dry, infection-controlled ear and assessment of hearing and Eustachian-tube function improve planning and outcome.
differentiation: Confirm a dry tympanic-membrane perforation by otomicroscopy and document conductive loss with audiometry and middle-ear status. Type-I tympanoplasty is appropriate when the ossicular chain is intact and no cholesteatoma or uncontrolled infection changes the operation.

title: Pneumatic otoscopy: visible tympanic-membrane mobility
subject: ENT / Pediatrics
source: Pneumatic Otoscopy | Channel: earsurgery
summary: With a sealed pneumatic speculum, gentle bulb pressure changes make the real tympanic membrane move inward and outward.
identify: The endoscopic view shows the tympanic membrane moving with applied pressure, rather than merely showing the examiner holding an otoscope.
examAngle: Normal mobility requires an airtight canal seal and a compliant tympanic membrane with an aerated middle ear. NEET PG pitfall: A leaking speculum seal can make a normal tympanic membrane appear falsely immobile.
management: Give analgesia and use age-, severity-, and risk-based observation or antibiotics for acute otitis media. Otitis media with effusion is usually observed with hearing follow-up; persistent bilateral hearing loss, speech risk, or structural change may require ventilation tubes.
quickRevision: At 00:03-00:18, watch for: With a sealed pneumatic speculum, gentle bulb pressure changes make the real tympanic membrane move inward and outward. Core concept: Normal mobility requires an airtight canal seal and a compliant tympanic membrane with an aerated middle ear. Pitfall: A leaking speculum seal can make a normal tympanic membrane appear falsely immobile.
anchors: Reduced or absent movement supports middle-ear effusion when the color, position, landmarks, and symptoms agree.
Use only small pressure changes and stop if the maneuver causes significant pain.
differentiation: Pneumatic otoscopy is interpreted with membrane color, position, translucency, landmarks, and mobility; tympanometry can support uncertain findings. A bulging poorly mobile membrane with acute symptoms favors acute otitis media, while neutral or retracted immobility may reflect effusion.

title: Cogan lid-twitch sign in myasthenia gravis
subject: Medicine / Neurology / Ophthalmology
source: Cogan lid twitch sign in myasthenia gravis | Channel: Dr Deeptha Pathirana
summary: After sustained downgaze, the upper lid briefly overshoots upward when the patient rapidly returns the eyes to primary position.

examAngle: Cogan lid twitch is a fatigability-related eyelid sign that supports ocular myasthenia gravis. NEET PG pitfall: A lid twitch is supportive, not diagnostic; thyroid eye disease and other eyelid disorders can mimic it.
management: Use pyridostigmine for symptomatic benefit and corticosteroid or steroid-sparing immunotherapy when needed; thymectomy is indicated for thymoma and selected generalized AChR-positive disease. Respiratory or bulbar deterioration is a myasthenic crisis requiring monitored ventilation and rapid immunotherapy.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: After sustained downgaze, the upper lid briefly overshoots upward when the patient rapidly returns the eyes to primary position. Core concept: Cogan lid twitch is a fatigability-related eyelid sign that supports ocular myasthenia gravis. Pitfall: A lid twitch is supportive, not diagnostic; thyroid eye disease and other eyelid disorders can mimic it.
anchors: Look for variable ptosis or diplopia and test fatigability; pupils remain normal in myasthenia.
AChR or MuSK antibodies, electrophysiology, and bedside ice testing are selected according to the clinical presentation.
differentiation: Look for fluctuating fatigable ptosis or diplopia with normal pupils, then use ice testing, AChR or MuSK antibodies, repetitive stimulation, or single-fiber EMG as appropriate. Evaluate thymic disease and screen for bulbar or respiratory weakness.

title: Endoscopic sphenopalatine-artery ligation for posterior epistaxis
subject: ENT
source: ESPAL : Endoscopic Sphenopalatine Artery Ligation / Cautery | Channel: Dr.Ajinkya H. Kedari
summary: The operative endoscopic view exposes the sphenopalatine region and controls the arterial branches for refractory posterior epistaxis.

examAngle: The sphenopalatine artery enters through the sphenopalatine foramen near the posterior middle turbinate. NEET PG pitfall: Recurrent epistaxis with a vascular nasopharyngeal mass in an adolescent boy requires imaging for angiofibroma, not blind biopsy.
management: Begin compression, topical vasoconstrictor and directed cautery when visible, then use packing as a bridge if necessary. Persistent posterior bleeding is treated by endoscopic sphenopalatine-artery ligation or angiographic embolization according to anatomy, risk, and expertise.
quickRevision: At 02:51-03:06, watch for: The operative endoscopic view exposes the sphenopalatine region and controls the arterial branches for refractory posterior epistaxis. Pitfall: Recurrent epistaxis with a vascular nasopharyngeal mass in an adolescent boy requires imaging for angiofibroma, not blind biopsy.
anchors: Clip or cauterize all identified branches and confirm hemostasis endoscopically.
This is a standard escalation after packing or conservative measures fail in posterior epistaxis.
differentiation: Posterior epistaxis is suspected with brisk bleeding into the pharynx, failure to identify an anterior point, or recurrence despite anterior measures. Stabilize first, correct anticoagulation or coagulopathy appropriately, and use endoscopy to identify the bleeding territory.

title: Endoscopic esophageal foreign-body retrieval: 3D procedural animation
subject: ENT / Pediatric Surgery

summary: The labeled 3D sequence shows an endoscopic snare-suction device approaching, securing, and withdrawing an esophageal foreign body.

examAngle: The cricopharyngeal region is the commonest site of esophageal foreign-body impaction. NEET PG pitfall: Do not induce vomiting or blindly advance an impacted sharp object or button battery.
management: Remove under endoscopic control with airway protection and inspect for mucosal injury. A button battery needs immediate extraction and protocol-based post-removal assessment for fistula or necrosis; do not induce vomiting or blindly push the object onward.
quickRevision: At 00:45-01:00, watch for: The labeled 3D sequence shows an endoscopic snare-suction device approaching, securing, and withdrawing an esophageal foreign body. Core concept: The cricopharyngeal region is the commonest site of esophageal foreign-body impaction. Pitfall: Do not induce vomiting or blindly advance an impacted sharp object or button battery.
