Extraction should maintain control of the object and protect the mucosa and airway during withdrawal.
differentiation: Locate the object and assess type, time, obstruction, perforation, and airway risk with radiographs or CT when appropriate. Esophageal button batteries, sharp objects, complete obstruction, and significant symptoms require emergency removal rather than observation.

title: Upper-eyelid eversion for subtarsal examination
subject: Ophthalmology
source: TFOS DEWSII Diagnostic Videos - Lid Eversion | Channel: TFOS
summary: The examiner asks the patient to look down, supports the upper tarsus, and everts the upper eyelid.
identify: The clip demonstrates the eversion maneuver and exposure of the upper tarsal conjunctiva; it does not claim that giant papillae are present.
examAngle: Place a cotton bud or fingertip above the superior tarsal border while gently pulling the lashes forward and upward. NEET PG pitfall: Do not evert an eyelid when an open-globe injury is suspected.
management: Remove a superficial foreign body under appropriate anesthesia and irrigation and treat any abrasion or infection risk. Giant papillary conjunctivitis requires stopping or replacing the offending lens or prosthesis, improved hygiene, lubrication, and topical anti-allergic therapy.
quickRevision: At 00:25-00:40, watch for: The examiner asks the patient to look down, supports the upper tarsus, and everts the upper eyelid. Core concept: Place a cotton bud or fingertip above the superior tarsal border while gently pulling the lashes forward and upward. Pitfall: Do not evert an eyelid when an open-globe injury is suspected.
anchors: Inspect the superior tarsal conjunctiva for a retained foreign body, papillae, follicles, or inflammation.
Recurrent vertical corneal abrasions are a clue to a hidden subtarsal foreign body.
differentiation: Evert the upper lid when a subtarsal foreign body, linear corneal abrasion, or giant papillary conjunctivitis is suspected. Inspect the superior tarsal conjunctiva, fornix, contact lens, cornea with fluorescein, and visual acuity before concluding the examination.

title: Parachute reaction in an infant
subject: Pediatrics / Developmental Neurology
source: Motor postural reflexes: parachute | Channel: Dr. Prodigious
summary: When the infant is moved rapidly toward a surface, both arms extend forward with open hands in a protective response.
identify: When the infant is moved rapidly toward a surface, both arms extend protectively; the anticipatory response is inherently dynamic.
examAngle: The parachute reaction is a protective extension response rather than a primitive reflex that later disappears. NEET PG pitfall: Moro abduction in a young infant is not the parachute reaction; parachute extension is directed toward the approaching surface.
management: A normally present protective extension needs no treatment. Delayed, absent, or asymmetric response requires developmental reassessment, evaluation for visual, vestibular, orthopedic, or neurologic disease, and early therapy when a delay is confirmed.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:14, watch for: When the infant is moved rapidly toward a surface, both arms extend forward with open hands in a protective response. Core concept: The parachute reaction is a protective extension response rather than a primitive reflex that later disappears. Pitfall: Moro abduction in a young infant is not the parachute reaction; parachute extension is directed toward the approaching surface.
anchors: It emerges at about eight to nine months and persists thereafter.
Absence, marked asymmetry, or delay should be interpreted with tone, vision, vestibular function, and overall motor development.
differentiation: Assess the parachute reaction near the expected developmental age with secure trunk support and no actual fall. Compare both arms and integrate vision, hearing, tone, head control, sitting, reaching, and other postural reactions.

title: Landau reflex in an infant
subject: Pediatrics / Developmental Neurology
source: Postural reflexes: Landau | Channel: Dr. Prodigious
summary: In horizontal ventral suspension, the infant lifts the head and extends the trunk and legs into a curved posture.
identify: In ventral suspension, the infant extends the head, trunk, and legs, and flexes them when the head is passively flexed; the postural sequence requires video.
examAngle: The Landau reaction reflects integration of labyrinthine righting and extensor tone. NEET PG pitfall: Judge the coordinated head-trunk-leg extension, not isolated head lifting produced by the examiner's grip.
management: An age-appropriate response requires no intervention. A persistently absent, asymmetric, or exaggerated response with developmental concern should trigger standardized developmental assessment and early pediatric neurologic and rehabilitation support.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:15, watch for: In horizontal ventral suspension, the infant lifts the head and extends the trunk and legs into a curved posture. Core concept: The Landau reaction reflects integration of labyrinthine righting and extensor tone. Pitfall: Judge the coordinated head-trunk-leg extension, not isolated head lifting produced by the examiner's grip.
anchors: It appears during early infancy, becomes well established by around three to four months, and fades during the second year.
An absent response may suggest hypotonia or motor delay, while an exaggerated persistent pattern may accompany hypertonia.
differentiation: Observe the coordinated Landau posture in ventral suspension and assess head control, axial tone, limb symmetry, primitive reflex integration, and milestones. Prematurity, drowsiness, hypotonia, and examiner support can alter the appearance.

title: Continuous curvilinear capsulorhexis: 3D procedural animation
subject: Ophthalmology
source: Cataract surgery Phacoemulsification and intraocular lens implantation | Channel: Omer Faruk Recep
summary: The animation isolates creation of a continuous circular anterior-capsule tear with controlled flap direction.
identify: The selected window shows forceps grasping and continuously tearing the anterior capsule into a circular opening; it does not claim to show the entire cataract operation.
examAngle: A continuous curvilinear capsulorhexis creates a strong smooth capsular edge for phacoemulsification and in-the-bag IOL support. NEET PG pitfall: The linked window demonstrates capsulorhexis only, not hydrodissection, phacoemulsification, or IOL insertion.
management: Maintain chamber stability and redirect the flap centrally; use capsular dye, decompression, pupil expansion, or capsular support when indicated. A radial extension requires immediate control and modification of hydrodissection, nucleus removal, and IOL placement.
quickRevision: At 01:53-02:00, watch for: The animation isolates creation of a continuous circular anterior-capsule tear with controlled flap direction. Core concept: A continuous curvilinear capsulorhexis creates a strong smooth capsular edge for phacoemulsification and in-the-bag IOL support. Pitfall: The linked window demonstrates capsulorhexis only, not hydrodissection, phacoemulsification, or IOL insertion.
anchors: The chamber must remain stable and the flap is redirected centrally if the tear begins to run peripherally.
An adequately sized centered opening overlaps the IOL optic and reduces radial-tear risk.
differentiation: During cataract surgery, a centered continuous capsulorhexis is judged by smoothness, diameter, and overlap of the intended IOL optic. Poor red reflex, intumescent lens, zonular weakness, a small pupil, or a tear running outward increases risk and changes technique.

title: Panretinal photocoagulation: peripheral laser-burn placement animation
subject: Ophthalmology
source: Proliferative Diabetic Retinopathy (PDR) Laser Photocoagulation | Channel: THE RETINA INSTITUTE
summary: Laser spots are placed sequentially across peripheral retina while the macula and optic disc are avoided.
identify: Laser spots visibly appear across the peripheral retina during the linked interval while the posterior pole remains spared.
examAngle: Panretinal photocoagulation reduces ischemic drive and VEGF production in proliferative diabetic retinopathy. NEET PG pitfall: PRP burns must not be placed through the foveal center merely because neovascularization is severe.
management: Panretinal photocoagulation is established treatment for proliferative disease, while intravitreal anti-VEGF is used alone or with laser in selected patients. Optimize glucose, blood pressure, renal and lipid risk, treat macular edema separately, and perform vitrectomy for nonclearing hemorrhage or traction threatening the macula.
quickRevision: At 00:20-00:35, watch for: Laser spots are placed sequentially across peripheral retina while the macula and optic disc are avoided. Core concept: Panretinal photocoagulation reduces ischemic drive and VEGF production in proliferative diabetic retinopathy. Pitfall: PRP burns must not be placed through the foveal center merely because neovascularization is severe.
anchors: Use appropriately spaced moderate-intensity burns outside the macula, often over more than one session.
Macular edema is assessed and treated separately, commonly with intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy.
differentiation: Diagnose proliferative diabetic retinopathy by retinal or disc neovascularization and assess macular edema with dilated examination and OCT. Fluorescein angiography can map leakage and nonperfusion when treatment planning is uncertain.

title: Nd:YAG capsulotomy for posterior capsular opacification
subject: Ophthalmology
source: YAG capsulotomy on my own eye for treatment of posterior capsule opacification. Surgeon's view. | Channel: Shannon Wong, MD
summary: The surgeon's-view segment shows laser delivery through the dilated pupil and progressive creation of a central posterior-capsule opening.
identify: The surgeon-view interval shows active laser delivery and progressive creation of the posterior-capsule opening without the patient-positioning lead-in.
examAngle: Nd:YAG capsulotomy treats visually significant posterior capsular opacification after cataract surgery. NEET PG pitfall: Confirm that reduced vision is due to posterior capsular opacity rather than retinal or optic-nerve disease before treatment.
management: Create a central Nd:YAG opening with the lowest effective energy while avoiding IOL pitting. Check pressure in at-risk patients, treat inflammation as needed, and warn about urgent review for flashes, floaters, a curtain, pain, or sudden visual loss.
quickRevision: At 05:28-05:40, watch for: The surgeon's-view segment shows laser delivery through the dilated pupil and progressive creation of a central posterior-capsule opening. Core concept: Nd:YAG capsulotomy treats visually significant posterior capsular opacification after cataract surgery. Pitfall: Confirm that reduced vision is due to posterior capsular opacity rather than retinal or optic-nerve disease before treatment.
anchors: Focus just posterior to the capsule and use the lowest effective energy to avoid IOL pitting.
Important complications include transient IOP rise, inflammation, cystoid macular edema, and retinal tear or detachment.
differentiation: Confirm that posterior capsular opacity, rather than retinal, corneal, refractive, or optic-nerve disease, explains reduced vision after cataract surgery. Slit-lamp examination should define the opacity, IOL position, pupil access, inflammation, and retinal risk.

title: Positive Seidel test: aqueous leak through a corneal wound
subject: Ophthalmology / Emergency Medicine
source: The waterfall in the eye: Seidel test | Channel: Dr. Biju Raju
summary: Cobalt-blue illumination reveals a bright diluted stream washing through fluorescein at the corneal wound, confirming aqueous leakage.
identify: Under cobalt-blue light, clear aqueous visibly dilutes and streams through fluorescein, producing a moving dark waterfall within the green dye.
examAngle: A positive Seidel test indicates a full-thickness corneal or limbal leak and possible open-globe injury. NEET PG pitfall: Do not press on the eye or perform tonometry when an open globe is suspected.
management: Place a rigid eye shield, keep the patient fasting, control pain and vomiting, give systemic broad-spectrum antibiotics and tetanus prophylaxis as indicated, and obtain immediate ophthalmic repair. Avoid tonometry, eyelid retraction, ultrasound pressure, or removal of a protruding object.
quickRevision: At 00:00-00:12, watch for: Cobalt-blue illumination reveals a bright diluted stream washing through fluorescein at the corneal wound, confirming aqueous leakage. Core concept: A positive Seidel test indicates a full-thickness corneal or limbal leak and possible open-globe injury. Pitfall: Do not press on the eye or perform tonometry when an open globe is suspected.
anchors: Protect the eye with a rigid shield, avoid pressure or manipulation, keep the patient fasting, and obtain urgent ophthalmic repair.
Systemic antibiotics, antiemesis, analgesia, and tetanus assessment are used as indicated; topical pressure testing is avoided.
differentiation: A positive Seidel stream confirms aqueous leakage, but a negative test does not exclude a self-sealed full-thickness wound. Assess visual acuity without pressure, pupil shape, anterior-chamber depth, uveal prolapse, and intraocular foreign-body risk, using CT rather than MRI when metal is possible.

title: Goldmann applanation tonometry: fluorescein semicircle endpoint
subject: Ophthalmology
source: An introduction to Goldmann Applanation Tonometry | Channel: Moorfields Eye Hospital NHS Foundation Trust
summary: The cobalt-blue view shows the two fluorescein semicircles moving as the Goldmann dial is adjusted until their inner borders just touch.
identify: The cobalt-blue examiner view shows the two fluorescein semicircles moving to the applanation endpoint, when their inner borders just touch.
examAngle: Correct Goldmann applanation tonometry endpoint is reached when the inner margins of the upper and lower fluorescein semicircles just meet at the midpoint of their pulsation. NEET PG pitfall: Judge the moving fluorescein-mire endpoint, not merely the patient's position at the slit lamp; poor fluorescein or squeezing can create a false reading.
management: Set an individualized target pressure and lower it with topical medication, laser trabeculoplasty, or surgery according to glaucoma type, damage, progression, and adherence. Acute angle closure requires emergency pressure reduction and definitive iridotomy after the cornea and angle permit.
quickRevision: At 05:15-05:20, watch for: The cobalt-blue view shows the two fluorescein semicircles moving as the Goldmann dial is adjusted until their inner borders just touch. Core concept: Correct Goldmann applanation tonometry endpoint is reached when the inner margins of the upper and lower fluorescein semicircles just meet at the midpoint of their pulsation. Pitfall: Judge the moving fluorescein-mire endpoint, not merely the patient's position at the slit lamp; poor fluorescein or squeezing can create a false reading.
anchors: The standard dial value is multiplied by ten to express intraocular pressure in mmHg.
Corneal thickness and biomechanics, astigmatism, excess or insufficient fluorescein, lid squeezing, and Valsalva effort can alter the result.
differentiation: Measure both eyes with correct fluorescein semicircles and repeat inconsistent readings. Interpret pressure with central corneal thickness, optic-disc and retinal-nerve-fiber appearance, gonioscopy, and visual fields; ocular hypertension is not synonymous with glaucoma.

title: Vesicoureteric reflux: retrograde urine-flow animation
subject: Surgery / Pediatrics / Radiodiagnosis
source: What is Vesicoureteral Reflux (VUR) - Animated Video | Channel: Deflux for VUR
summary: The medical animation shows urine moving backward from the bladder through the ureters toward the kidneys instead of remaining unidirectional.
identify: The short animation shows fluid moving backward from the bladder up the ureters toward the kidneys; the direction of flow is the tested feature.
examAngle: Vesicoureteric reflux is retrograde urine flow from the bladder into the ureter and sometimes the renal collecting system. NEET PG pitfall: This clip is a mechanism animation, not patient fluoroscopy, so it demonstrates the direction of reflux but cannot establish a patient-specific VCUG grade.
management: Treat bladder-bowel dysfunction and prevent recurrent febrile infection; observation or selective antimicrobial prophylaxis is used in many lower-risk children. Recurrent breakthrough infection, high-grade persistent reflux, or renal risk may require endoscopic injection or ureteric reimplantation.
quickRevision: At 00:24-00:34, watch for: The medical animation shows urine moving backward from the bladder through the ureters toward the kidneys instead of remaining unidirectional. Core concept: Vesicoureteric reflux is retrograde urine flow from the bladder into the ureter and sometimes the renal collecting system. Pitfall: This clip is a mechanism animation, not patient fluoroscopy, so it demonstrates the direction of reflux but cannot establish a patient-specific VCUG grade.
anchors: VCUG demonstrates reflux during filling or voiding and grades it I to V by proximal extent, dilatation, and tortuosity.
Recurrent febrile urinary infection, renal scarring, bladder-bowel dysfunction, age, sex, and reflux grade guide investigation and management.
differentiation: VCUG is selected after the appropriate urinary-infection and ultrasound assessment to demonstrate reflux during filling and voiding, grade it, and inspect the urethra. Interpret grade with age, sex, renal scarring, bladder-bowel dysfunction, breakthrough infection, and kidney function.

title: Mid-upper-arm circumference measurement for severe acute malnutrition
subject: Community Medicine / Pediatrics
source: Outpatient Therapeutic Feeding Training Video - Screening for Acute Malnutrition | Channel: UNICEF Ethiopia
summary: The field demonstration locates the arm midpoint, wraps a color-coded MUAC tape without compressing tissue, and reads the value.
identify: The midpoint must be located before a noncompressive tape is wrapped and the color zone or circumference is read.
examAngle: Measure the midpoint between acromion and olecranon, conventionally on the left arm. NEET PG pitfall: A loose or tissue-compressing tape can move a child across a treatment threshold.
management: Uncomplicated severe acute malnutrition with appetite is managed in an outpatient therapeutic program using ready-to-use therapeutic food and close follow-up. Failed appetite or medical complications require inpatient stabilization with cautious feeding, infection treatment, electrolyte and temperature care, then rehabilitation.
quickRevision: At 01:28-01:43, watch for: The field demonstration locates the arm midpoint, wraps a color-coded MUAC tape without compressing tissue, and reads the value. Core concept: Measure the midpoint between acromion and olecranon, conventionally on the left arm. Pitfall: A loose or tissue-compressing tape can move a child across a treatment threshold.
anchors: For children 6 to 59 months, MUAC below 11.5 cm indicates severe acute malnutrition.
Bilateral pitting edema also identifies severe acute malnutrition regardless of MUAC.
differentiation: For children 6-59 months, MUAC below 11.5 cm or bilateral pitting edema identifies severe acute malnutrition, while weight-for-height and clinical complications complete classification. Assess appetite, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, dehydration, infection, anemia, and feeding safety.
